1.play: KING Henry VI

2.play: Edward, Son to the King, and Prince of Wales

3.play: Duke of Somerset

4.play: Earl of Northumberland

5.play: Earl of Oxford

6.play: Earl of Exeter

7.play: Lord Clifford

8.play: Earl of Richmond, a Youth, afterwards King Henry VII

9.play: Richard, Duke of York

10.play: Edward, eldest Son to the Duke of York, afterwards King Edward IV

11.play: George, Duke of Clarence, second Son to the Duke of York

12.play: Richard, Duke of Gloucester, third Son to the Duke of York, afterwards King Richard III

13.play: Edmund, Earl of Rutland, youngest Son to the Duke of York

14.play: Duke of Norfolk

15.play: Marquis of Montague

16.play: Earl of Warwick

17.play: Earl of Salisbury

18.play: Earl of Pembroke

19.play: Lord Hastings

20.play: Lord Stafford

21.play: Sir John Montacute

22.play: Sir Hugh Montacute

23.play: Sir William Stauley, afterwards Earl of Derby

24.play: Lord Rivet, Brother to the Lady Grey

25.play: Sir John Montgomery

26.play: Lieutenant of the Tower

27.play: Mayor of Coventry

28.play: Mayor and Aldermen of York

29.play: Humphrey and Sutton, two Hunsfords

30.play: Lewis King of France

31.play: Bourbon, Admiral of France

32.play: Queen Margaret

33.play: Bona, Sister to the French King

34.play: Lady Grey, Widow of Sir Richard Grey, afterwards Queen to Edward IV

35.play: Soldiers and other Attendants on King Henry and King Edward.

36.play: Scene: In Part of the Third Act the Scene is laid in France, during all the rest of the Play in England.

1.play: Alarum. Enter YORK, EDWARD, RICHARD, NORFOLK, MONTAGUE, WARWICK, and Soldiers

2.play: WARWICK: I wonder how the king escaped our hands.

3.play: YORK: While we pursued the horsemen of the north,

4.play: He slily stole away and left his men:

5.play: Whereat the great Lord of Northumberland,

6.play: Whose warlike ears could never brook retreat,

7.play: Cheer'd up the drooping army; and himself,

8.play: Lord Clifford and Lord Stafford, all abreast,

9.play: Charged our main battle's front, and breaking in

10.play: Were by the swords of common soldiers slain.

11.play: EDWARD: Lord Stafford's father, Duke of Buckingham,

12.play: Is either slain or wounded dangerously;

13.play: I cleft his beaver with a downright blow:

14.play: That this is true, father, behold his blood.

15.play: MONTAGUE: And, brother, here's the Earl of Wiltshire's blood,

16.play: Whom I encounter'd as the battles join'd.

17.play: RICHARD: Speak thou for me and tell them what I did.

18.play: YORK: Richard hath best deserved of all my sons.

19.play: But is your grace dead, my Lord of Somerset?

20.play: NORFOLK: Such hope have all the line of John of Gaunt!

21.play: RICHARD: Thus do I hope to shake King Henry's head.

22.play: WARWICK: And so do I. Victorious Prince of York,

23.play: Before I see thee seated in that throne

24.play: Which now the house of Lancaster usurps,

25.play: I vow by heaven these eyes shall never close.

26.play: This is the palace of the fearful king,

27.play: And this the regal seat: possess it, York;

28.play: For this is thine and not King Henry's heirs'

29.play: YORK: Assist me, then, sweet Warwick, and I will;

30.play: For hither we have broken in by force.

31.play: NORFOLK: We'll all assist you; he that flies shall die.

32.play: YORK: Thanks, gentle Norfolk: stay by me, my lords;

33.play: And, soldiers, stay and lodge by me this night.

34.play: WARWICK: And when the king comes, offer no violence,

35.play: Unless he seek to thrust you out perforce.

36.play: YORK: The queen this day here holds her parliament,

37.play: But little thinks we shall be of her council:

38.play: By words or blows here let us win our right.

39.play: RICHARD: Arm'd as we are, let's stay within this house.

40.play: WARWICK: The bloody parliament shall this be call'd,

41.play: Unless Plantagenet, Duke of York, be king,

42.play: And bashful Henry deposed, whose cowardice

43.play: Hath made us by-words to our enemies.

44.play: YORK: Then leave me not, my lords; be resolute;

45.play: I mean to take possession of my right.

46.play: WARWICK: Neither the king, nor he that loves him best,

47.play: The proudest he that holds up Lancaster,

48.play: Dares stir a wing, if Warwick shake his bells.

49.play: I'll plant Plantagenet, root him up who dares:

50.play: Resolve thee, Richard; claim the English crown.

51.play: KING HENRY VI: My lords, look where the sturdy rebel sits,

52.play: Even in the chair of state: belike he means,

53.play: Back'd by the power of Warwick, that false peer,

54.play: To aspire unto the crown and reign as king.

55.play: Earl of Northumberland, he slew thy father.

56.play: And thine, Lord Clifford; and you both have vow'd revenge

57.play: On him, his sons, his favourites and his friends.

58.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: If I be not, heavens be revenged on me!

59.play: CLIFFORD: The hope thereof makes Clifford mourn in steel.

60.play: WESTMORELAND: What, shall we suffer this? let's pluck him down:

61.play: My heart for anger burns; I cannot brook it.

62.play: KING HENRY VI: Be patient, gentle Earl of Westmoreland.

63.play: CLIFFORD: Patience is for poltroons, such as he:

64.play: He durst not sit there, had your father lived.

65.play: My gracious lord, here in the parliament

66.play: Let us assail the family of York.

67.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Well hast thou spoken, cousin: be it so.

68.play: KING HENRY VI: Ah, know you not the city favours them,

69.play: And they have troops of soldiers at their beck?

70.play: EXETER: But when the duke is slain, they'll quickly fly.

71.play: KING HENRY VI: Far be the thought of this from Henry's heart,

72.play: To make a shambles of the parliament-house!

73.play: Cousin of Exeter, frowns, words and threats

74.play: Shall be the war that Henry means to use.

75.play: Thou factious Duke of York, descend my throne,

76.play: and kneel for grace and mercy at my feet;

79.play: EXETER: For shame, come down: he made thee Duke of York.

80.play: YORK: 'Twas my inheritance, as the earldom was.

81.play: EXETER: Thy father was a traitor to the crown.

82.play: WARWICK: Exeter, thou art a traitor to the crown

84.play: CLIFFORD: Whom should he follow but his natural king?

85.play: WARWICK: True, Clifford; and that's Richard Duke of York.

86.play: KING HENRY VI: And shall I stand, and thou sit in my throne?

87.play: YORK: It must and shall be so: content thyself.

88.play: WARWICK: Be Duke of Lancaster; let him be king.

89.play: WESTMORELAND: He is both king and Duke of Lancaster;

90.play: And that the Lord of Westmoreland shall maintain.

91.play: WARWICK: And Warwick shall disprove it. You forget

92.play: That we are those which chased you from the field

93.play: And slew your fathers, and with colours spread

94.play: March'd through the city to the palace gates.

95.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Yes, Warwick, I remember it to my grief;

96.play: And, by his soul, thou and thy house shall rue it.

97.play: WESTMORELAND: Plantagenet, of thee and these thy sons,

98.play: Thy kinsman and thy friends, I'll have more lives

99.play: Than drops of blood were in my father's veins.

100.play: CLIFFORD: Urge it no more; lest that, instead of words,

101.play: I send thee, Warwick, such a messenger

102.play: As shall revenge his death before I stir.

103.play: WARWICK: Poor Clifford! how I scorn his worthless threats!

104.play: YORK: Will you we show our title to the crown?

105.play: If not, our swords shall plead it in the field.

106.play: KING HENRY VI: What title hast thou, traitor, to the crown?

107.play: Thy father was, as thou art, Duke of York;

108.play: Thy grandfather, Roger Mortimer, Earl of March:

109.play: I am the son of Henry the Fifth,

110.play: Who made the Dauphin and the French to stoop

111.play: And seized upon their towns and provinces.

112.play: WARWICK: Talk not of France, sith thou hast lost it all.

113.play: KING HENRY VI: The lord protector lost it, and not I:

114.play: When I was crown'd I was but nine months old.

115.play: RICHARD: You are old enough now, and yet, methinks, you lose.

116.play: Father, tear the crown from the usurper's head.

117.play: EDWARD: Sweet father, do so; set it on your head.

118.play: MONTAGUE: Good brother, as thou lovest and honourest arms,

119.play: Let's fight it out and not stand cavilling thus.

120.play: RICHARD: Sound drums and trumpets, and the king will fly.

122.play: KING HENRY VI: Peace, thou! and give King Henry leave to speak.

123.play: WARWICK: Plantagenet shall speak first: hear him, lords;

124.play: And be you silent and attentive too,

125.play: For he that interrupts him shall not live.

126.play: KING HENRY VI: Think'st thou that I will leave my kingly throne,

127.play: Wherein my grandsire and my father sat?

128.play: No: first shall war unpeople this my realm;

129.play: Ay, and their colours, often borne in France,

130.play: And now in England to our heart's great sorrow,

131.play: Shall be my winding-sheet. Why faint you, lords?

132.play: My title's good, and better far than his.

133.play: WARWICK: Prove it, Henry, and thou shalt be king.

134.play: KING HENRY VI: Henry the Fourth by conquest got the crown.

135.play: YORK: 'Twas by rebellion against his king.

136.play: KING HENRY VI: [Aside]  I know not what to say; my title's weak.--

137.play: Tell me, may not a king adopt an heir?

139.play: KING HENRY VI: An if he may, then am I lawful king;

140.play: For Richard, in the view of many lords,

141.play: Resign'd the crown to Henry the Fourth,

142.play: Whose heir my father was, and I am his.

143.play: YORK: He rose against him, being his sovereign,

144.play: And made him to resign his crown perforce.

145.play: WARWICK: Suppose, my lords, he did it unconstrain'd,

146.play: Think you 'twere prejudicial to his crown?

147.play: EXETER: No; for he could not so resign his crown

148.play: But that the next heir should succeed and reign.

149.play: KING HENRY VI: Art thou against us, Duke of Exeter?

150.play: EXETER: His is the right, and therefore pardon me.

151.play: YORK: Why whisper you, my lords, and answer not?

152.play: EXETER: My conscience tells me he is lawful king.

153.play: KING HENRY VI: [Aside]  All will revolt from me, and turn to him.

154.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Plantagenet, for all the claim thou lay'st,

155.play: Think not that Henry shall be so deposed.

156.play: WARWICK: Deposed he shall be, in despite of all.

157.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Thou art deceived: 'tis not thy southern power,

158.play: Of Essex, Norfolk, Suffolk, nor of Kent,

159.play: Which makes thee thus presumptuous and proud,

160.play: Can set the duke up in despite of me.

161.play: CLIFFORD: King Henry, be thy title right or wrong,

162.play: Lord Clifford vows to fight in thy defence:

163.play: May that ground gape and swallow me alive,

164.play: Where I shall kneel to him that slew my father!

165.play: KING HENRY VI: O Clifford, how thy words revive my heart!

166.play: YORK: Henry of Lancaster, resign thy crown.

167.play: What mutter you, or what conspire you, lords?

168.play: WARWICK: Do right unto this princely Duke of York,

169.play: Or I will fill the house with armed men,

170.play: And over the chair of state, where now he sits,

171.play: Write up his title with usurping blood.

172.play: KING HENRY VI: My Lord of Warwick, hear me but one word:

173.play: Let me for this my life-time reign as king.

174.play: YORK: Confirm the crown to me and to mine heirs,

175.play: And thou shalt reign in quiet while thou livest.

176.play: KING HENRY VI: I am content: Richard Plantagenet,

177.play: Enjoy the kingdom after my decease.

178.play: CLIFFORD: What wrong is this unto the prince your son!

179.play: WARWICK: What good is this to England and himself!

180.play: WESTMORELAND: Base, fearful and despairing Henry!

181.play: CLIFFORD: How hast thou injured both thyself and us!

182.play: WESTMORELAND: I cannot stay to hear these articles.

184.play: CLIFFORD: Come, cousin, let us tell the queen these news.

185.play: WESTMORELAND: Farewell, faint-hearted and degenerate king,

186.play: In whose cold blood no spark of honour bides.

187.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Be thou a prey unto the house of York,

188.play: And die in bands for this unmanly deed!

189.play: CLIFFORD: In dreadful war mayst thou be overcome,

190.play: Or live in peace abandon'd and despised!

191.play: WARWICK: Turn this way, Henry, and regard them not.

192.play: EXETER: They seek revenge and therefore will not yield.

194.play: WARWICK: Why should you sigh, my lord?

195.play: KING HENRY VI: Not for myself, Lord Warwick, but my son,

197.play: But be it as it may: I here entail

198.play: The crown to thee and to thine heirs for ever;

199.play: Conditionally, that here thou take an oath

200.play: To cease this civil war, and, whilst I live,

201.play: To honour me as thy king and sovereign,

202.play: And neither by treason nor hostility

203.play: To seek to put me down and reign thyself.

204.play: YORK: This oath I willingly take and will perform.

205.play: WARWICK: Long live King Henry! Plantagenet embrace him.

206.play: KING HENRY VI: And long live thou and these thy forward sons!

207.play: YORK: Now York and Lancaster are reconciled.

208.play: EXETER: Accursed be he that seeks to make them foes!

209.play: YORK: Farewell, my gracious lord; I'll to my castle.

210.play: WARWICK: And I'll keep London with my soldiers.

211.play: NORFOLK: And I to Norfolk with my followers.

212.play: MONTAGUE: And I unto the sea from whence I came.

213.play: KING HENRY VI: And I, with grief and sorrow, to the court.

214.play: EXETER: Here comes the queen, whose looks bewray her anger:

216.play: KING HENRY VI: Exeter, so will I.

217.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Nay, go not from me; I will follow thee.

218.play: KING HENRY VI: Be patient, gentle queen, and I will stay.

219.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Who can be patient in such extremes?

220.play: Ah, wretched man! would I had died a maid

221.play: And never seen thee, never borne thee son,

222.play: Seeing thou hast proved so unnatural a father

223.play: Hath he deserved to lose his birthright thus?

224.play: Hadst thou but loved him half so well as I,

225.play: Or felt that pain which I did for him once,

226.play: Or nourish'd him as I did with my blood,

227.play: Thou wouldst have left thy dearest heart-blood there,

228.play: Rather than have that savage duke thine heir

230.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Father, you cannot disinherit me:

231.play: If you be king, why should not I succeed?

232.play: KING HENRY VI: Pardon me, Margaret; pardon me, sweet son:

233.play: The Earl of Warwick and the duke enforced me.

234.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Enforced thee! art thou king, and wilt be forced?

235.play: I shame to hear thee speak. Ah, timorous wretch!

236.play: Thou hast undone thyself, thy son and me;

237.play: And given unto the house of York such head

238.play: As thou shalt reign but by their sufferance.

239.play: To entail him and his heirs unto the crown,

240.play: What is it, but to make thy sepulchre

241.play: And creep into it far before thy time?

242.play: Warwick is chancellor and the lord of Calais;

243.play: Stern Falconbridge commands the narrow seas;

244.play: The duke is made protector of the realm;

245.play: And yet shalt thou be safe? such safety finds

246.play: The trembling lamb environed with wolves.

247.play: Had I been there, which am a silly woman,

248.play: The soldiers should have toss'd me on their pikes

249.play: Before I would have granted to that act.

250.play: But thou preferr'st thy life before thine honour:

251.play: And seeing thou dost, I here divorce myself

252.play: Both from thy table, Henry, and thy bed,

253.play: Until that act of parliament be repeal'd

255.play: The northern lords that have forsworn thy colours

256.play: Will follow mine, if once they see them spread;

257.play: And spread they shall be, to thy foul disgrace

258.play: And utter ruin of the house of York.

259.play: Thus do I leave thee. Come, son, let's away;

260.play: Our army is ready; come, we'll after them.

261.play: KING HENRY VI: Stay, gentle Margaret, and hear me speak.

262.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Thou hast spoke too much already: get thee gone.

263.play: KING HENRY VI: Gentle son Edward, thou wilt stay with me?

264.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Ay, to be murder'd by his enemies.

265.play: PRINCE EDWARD: When I return with victory from the field

266.play: I'll see your grace: till then I'll follow her.

267.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Come, son, away; we may not linger thus.

268.play: KING HENRY VI: Poor queen! how love to me and to her son

269.play: Hath made her break out into terms of rage!

270.play: Revenged may she be on that hateful duke,

271.play: Whose haughty spirit, winged with desire,

272.play: Will cost my crown, and like an empty eagle

273.play: Tire on the flesh of me and of my son!

274.play: The loss of those three lords torments my heart:

275.play: I'll write unto them and entreat them fair.

276.play: Come, cousin you shall be the messenger.

277.play: EXETER: And I, I hope, shall reconcile them all.

1.play: Enter RICHARD, EDWARD, and MONTAGUE

2.play: RICHARD: Brother, though I be youngest, give me leave.

3.play: EDWARD: No, I can better play the orator.

4.play: MONTAGUE: But I have reasons strong and forcible.

5.play: YORK: Why, how now, sons and brother! at a strife?

6.play: What is your quarrel? how began it first?

7.play: EDWARD: No quarrel, but a slight contention.

8.play: YORK: About what?

9.play: RICHARD: About that which concerns your grace and us;

10.play: The crown of England, father, which is yours.

11.play: YORK: Mine boy? not till King Henry be dead.

12.play: RICHARD: Your right depends not on his life or death.

13.play: EDWARD: Now you are heir, therefore enjoy it now:

14.play: By giving the house of Lancaster leave to breathe,

15.play: It will outrun you, father, in the end.

16.play: YORK: I took an oath that he should quietly reign.

17.play: EDWARD: But for a kingdom any oath may be broken:

18.play: I would break a thousand oaths to reign one year.

19.play: RICHARD: No; God forbid your grace should be forsworn.

20.play: YORK: I shall be, if I claim by open war.

21.play: RICHARD: I'll prove the contrary, if you'll hear me speak.

22.play: YORK: Thou canst not, son; it is impossible.

23.play: RICHARD: An oath is of no moment, being not took

24.play: Before a true and lawful magistrate,

25.play: That hath authority over him that swears:

26.play: Henry had none, but did usurp the place;

27.play: Then, seeing 'twas he that made you to depose,

28.play: Your oath, my lord, is vain and frivolous.

29.play: Therefore, to arms! And, father, do but think

30.play: How sweet a thing it is to wear a crown;

31.play: Within whose circuit is Elysium

32.play: And all that poets feign of bliss and joy.

33.play: Why do we finger thus? I cannot rest

34.play: Until the white rose that I wear be dyed

35.play: Even in the lukewarm blood of Henry's heart.

36.play: YORK: Richard, enough; I will be king, or die.

37.play: Brother, thou shalt to London presently,

38.play: And whet on Warwick to this enterprise.

39.play: Thou, Richard, shalt to the Duke of Norfolk,

40.play: And tell him privily of our intent.

41.play: You Edward, shall unto my Lord Cobham,

42.play: With whom the Kentishmen will willingly rise:

43.play: In them I trust; for they are soldiers,

44.play: Witty, courteous, liberal, full of spirit.

45.play: While you are thus employ'd, what resteth more,

46.play: But that I seek occasion how to rise,

47.play: And yet the king not privy to my drift,

48.play: Nor any of the house of Lancaster?

49.play: But, stay: what news? Why comest thou in such post?

50.play: Ay, with my sword. What! think'st thou that we fear them?

51.play: Edward and Richard, you shall stay with me;

52.play: My brother Montague shall post to London:

53.play: Let noble Warwick, Cobham, and the rest,

54.play: Whom we have left protectors of the king,

56.play: And trust not simple Henry nor his oaths.

57.play: MONTAGUE: Brother, I go; I'll win them, fear it not:

58.play: And thus most humbly I do take my leave.

59.play: Sir John and Sir Hugh Mortimer, mine uncles,

60.play: You are come to Sandal in a happy hour;

61.play: The army of the queen mean to besiege us.

62.play: JOHN MORTIMER: She shall not need; we'll meet her in the field.

63.play: YORK: What, with five thousand men?

64.play: RICHARD: Ay, with five hundred, father, for a need:

65.play: A woman's general; what should we fear?

66.play: EDWARD: I hear their drums: let's set our men in order,

67.play: And issue forth and bid them battle straight.

68.play: YORK: Five men to twenty! though the odds be great,

69.play: I doubt not, uncle, of our victory.

70.play: Many a battle have I won in France,

71.play: When as the enemy hath been ten to one:

72.play: Why should I not now have the like success?

1.play: Alarums. Enter RUTLAND and his Tutor

2.play: RUTLAND: Ah, whither shall I fly to 'scape their hands?

3.play: Ah, tutor, look where bloody Clifford comes!

4.play: CLIFFORD: Chaplain, away! thy priesthood saves thy life.

5.play: As for the brat of this accursed duke,

6.play: Whose father slew my father, he shall die.

7.play: Soldiers, away with him!

8.play: Exit, dragged off by Soldiers

9.play: CLIFFORD: How now! is he dead already? or is it fear

10.play: That makes him close his eyes? I'll open them.

11.play: RUTLAND: So looks the pent-up lion o'er the wretch

12.play: That trembles under his devouring paws;

13.play: And so he walks, insulting o'er his prey,

14.play: And so he comes, to rend his limbs asunder.

15.play: Ah, gentle Clifford, kill me with thy sword,

16.play: And not with such a cruel threatening look.

17.play: Sweet Clifford, hear me speak before I die.

18.play: I am too mean a subject for thy wrath:

19.play: Be thou revenged on men, and let me live.

20.play: CLIFFORD: In vain thou speak'st, poor boy; my father's blood

21.play: Hath stopp'd the passage where thy words should enter.

22.play: RUTLAND: Then let my father's blood open it again:

23.play: He is a man, and, Clifford, cope with him.

24.play: CLIFFORD: Had thy brethren here, their lives and thine

25.play: Were not revenge sufficient for me;

26.play: No, if I digg'd up thy forefathers' graves

27.play: And hung their rotten coffins up in chains,

28.play: It could not slake mine ire, nor ease my heart.

29.play: The sight of any of the house of York

30.play: Is as a fury to torment my soul;

31.play: And till I root out their accursed line

32.play: And leave not one alive, I live in hell.

33.play: Therefore--

34.play: RUTLAND: O, let me pray before I take my death!

35.play: To thee I pray; sweet Clifford, pity me!

36.play: CLIFFORD: Such pity as my rapier's point affords.

37.play: RUTLAND: I never did thee harm: why wilt thou slay me?

38.play: CLIFFORD: Thy father hath.

39.play: RUTLAND: But 'twas ere I was born.

40.play: Thou hast one son; for his sake pity me,

41.play: Lest in revenge thereof, sith God is just,

42.play: He be as miserably slain as I.

43.play: Ah, let me live in prison all my days;

44.play: And when I give occasion of offence,

45.play: Then let me die, for now thou hast no cause.

46.play: CLIFFORD: No cause!

47.play: Thy father slew my father; therefore, die.

48.play: RUTLAND: Di faciant laudis summa sit ista tuae!

49.play: CLIFFORD: Plantagenet! I come, Plantagenet!

50.play: And this thy son's blood cleaving to my blade

51.play: Shall rust upon my weapon, till thy blood,

52.play: Congeal'd with this, do make me wipe off both.

1.play: Alarum. Enter YORK

2.play: YORK: The army of the queen hath got the field:

3.play: My uncles both are slain in rescuing me;

4.play: And all my followers to the eager foe

5.play: Turn back and fly, like ships before the wind

6.play: Or lambs pursued by hunger-starved wolves.

7.play: My sons, God knows what hath bechanced them:

8.play: But this I know, they have demean'd themselves

9.play: Like men born to renown by life or death.

10.play: Three times did Richard make a lane to me.

11.play: And thrice cried 'Courage, father! fight it out!'

12.play: And full as oft came Edward to my side,

13.play: With purple falchion, painted to the hilt

14.play: In blood of those that had encounter'd him:

15.play: And when the hardiest warriors did retire,

16.play: Richard cried 'Charge! and give no foot of ground!'

17.play: And cried 'A crown, or else a glorious tomb!

18.play: A sceptre, or an earthly sepulchre!'

19.play: With this, we charged again: but, out, alas!

20.play: We bodged again; as I have seen a swan

21.play: With bootless labour swim against the tide

22.play: And spend her strength with over-matching waves.

23.play: Ah, hark! the fatal followers do pursue;

24.play: And I am faint and cannot fly their fury:

25.play: And were I strong, I would not shun their fury:

26.play: The sands are number'd that make up my life;

27.play: Here must I stay, and here my life must end.

28.play: Come, bloody Clifford, rough Northumberland,

29.play: I dare your quenchless fury to more rage:

30.play: I am your butt, and I abide your shot.

31.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Yield to our mercy, proud Plantagenet.

32.play: CLIFFORD: Ay, to such mercy as his ruthless arm,

33.play: With downright payment, show'd unto my father.

34.play: Now Phaethon hath tumbled from his car,

35.play: And made an evening at the noontide prick.

36.play: YORK: My ashes, as the phoenix, may bring forth

37.play: A bird that will revenge upon you all:

38.play: And in that hope I throw mine eyes to heaven,

39.play: Scorning whate'er you can afflict me with.

40.play: Why come you not? what! multitudes, and fear?

41.play: CLIFFORD: So cowards fight when they can fly no further;

42.play: So doves do peck the falcon's piercing talons;

43.play: So desperate thieves, all hopeless of their lives,

44.play: Breathe out invectives 'gainst the officers.

45.play: YORK: O Clifford, but bethink thee once again,

46.play: And in thy thought o'er-run my former time;

47.play: And, if though canst for blushing, view this face,

48.play: And bite thy tongue, that slanders him with cowardice

49.play: Whose frown hath made thee faint and fly ere this!

50.play: CLIFFORD: I will not bandy with thee word for word,

51.play: But buckle with thee blows, twice two for one.

52.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Hold, valiant Clifford! for a thousand causes

53.play: I would prolong awhile the traitor's life.

54.play: Wrath makes him deaf: speak thou, Northumberland.

55.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Hold, Clifford! do not honour him so much

56.play: To prick thy finger, though to wound his heart:

57.play: What valour were it, when a cur doth grin,

58.play: For one to thrust his hand between his teeth,

59.play: When he might spurn him with his foot away?

60.play: It is war's prize to take all vantages;

61.play: And ten to one is no impeach of valour.

62.play: CLIFFORD: Ay, ay, so strives the woodcock with the gin.

63.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: So doth the cony struggle in the net.

64.play: YORK: So triumph thieves upon their conquer'd booty;

65.play: So true men yield, with robbers so o'ermatch'd.

66.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: What would your grace have done unto him now?

67.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Brave warriors, Clifford and Northumberland,

68.play: Come, make him stand upon this molehill here,

69.play: That raught at mountains with outstretched arms,

70.play: Yet parted but the shadow with his hand.

71.play: What! was it you that would be England's king?

72.play: Was't you that revell'd in our parliament,

73.play: And made a preachment of your high descent?

74.play: Where are your mess of sons to back you now?

75.play: The wanton Edward, and the lusty George?

76.play: And where's that valiant crook-back prodigy,

77.play: Dicky your boy, that with his grumbling voice

78.play: Was wont to cheer his dad in mutinies?

79.play: Or, with the rest, where is your darling Rutland?

80.play: Look, York: I stain'd this napkin with the blood

81.play: That valiant Clifford, with his rapier's point,

82.play: Made issue from the bosom of the boy;

83.play: And if thine eyes can water for his death,

84.play: I give thee this to dry thy cheeks withal.

85.play: Alas poor York! but that I hate thee deadly,

86.play: I should lament thy miserable state.

87.play: I prithee, grieve, to make me merry, York.

88.play: What, hath thy fiery heart so parch'd thine entrails

89.play: That not a tear can fall for Rutland's death?

90.play: Why art thou patient, man? thou shouldst be mad;

91.play: And I, to make thee mad, do mock thee thus.

92.play: Stamp, rave, and fret, that I may sing and dance.

93.play: Thou wouldst be fee'd, I see, to make me sport:

94.play: York cannot speak, unless he wear a crown.

95.play: A crown for York! and, lords, bow low to him:

96.play: Hold you his hands, whilst I do set it on.

97.play: Ay, marry, sir, now looks he like a king!

98.play: Ay, this is he that took King Henry's chair,

99.play: And this is he was his adopted heir.

100.play: But how is it that great Plantagenet

101.play: Is crown'd so soon, and broke his solemn oath?

102.play: As I bethink me, you should not be king

103.play: Till our King Henry had shook hands with death.

104.play: And will you pale your head in Henry's glory,

105.play: And rob his temples of the diadem,

106.play: Now in his life, against your holy oath?

107.play: O, 'tis a fault too too unpardonable!

108.play: Off with the crown, and with the crown his head;

109.play: And, whilst we breathe, take time to do him dead.

110.play: CLIFFORD: That is my office, for my father's sake.

111.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Nay, stay; lets hear the orisons he makes.

112.play: YORK: She-wolf of France, but worse than wolves of France,

113.play: Whose tongue more poisons than the adder's tooth!

114.play: How ill-beseeming is it in thy sex

115.play: To triumph, like an Amazonian trull,

116.play: Upon their woes whom fortune captivates!

117.play: But that thy face is, vizard-like, unchanging,

118.play: Made impudent with use of evil deeds,

119.play: I would assay, proud queen, to make thee blush.

120.play: To tell thee whence thou camest, of whom derived,

121.play: Were shame enough to shame thee, wert thou not shameless.

122.play: Thy father bears the type of King of Naples,

123.play: Of both the Sicils and Jerusalem,

124.play: Yet not so wealthy as an English yeoman.

125.play: Hath that poor monarch taught thee to insult?

126.play: It needs not, nor it boots thee not, proud queen,

127.play: Unless the adage must be verified,

128.play: That beggars mounted run their horse to death.

129.play: 'Tis beauty that doth oft make women proud;

130.play: But, God he knows, thy share thereof is small:

131.play: 'Tis virtue that doth make them most admired;

132.play: The contrary doth make thee wonder'd at:

133.play: 'Tis government that makes them seem divine;

134.play: The want thereof makes thee abominable:

135.play: Thou art as opposite to every good

136.play: As the Antipodes are unto us,

137.play: Or as the south to the septentrion.

138.play: O tiger's heart wrapt in a woman's hide!

139.play: How couldst thou drain the life-blood of the child,

140.play: To bid the father wipe his eyes withal,

141.play: And yet be seen to bear a woman's face?

142.play: Women are soft, mild, pitiful and flexible;

143.play: Thou stern, obdurate, flinty, rough, remorseless.

144.play: Bids't thou me rage? why, now thou hast thy wish:

145.play: Wouldst have me weep? why, now thou hast thy will:

146.play: For raging wind blows up incessant showers,

147.play: And when the rage allays, the rain begins.

148.play: These tears are my sweet Rutland's obsequies:

149.play: And every drop cries vengeance for his death,

150.play: 'Gainst thee, fell Clifford, and thee, false

152.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Beshrew me, but his passion moves me so

153.play: That hardly can I cheque my eyes from tears.

154.play: YORK: That face of his the hungry cannibals

155.play: Would not have touch'd, would not have stain'd with blood:

156.play: But you are more inhuman, more inexorable,

157.play: O, ten times more, than tigers of Hyrcania.

158.play: See, ruthless queen, a hapless father's tears:

159.play: This cloth thou dip'dst in blood of my sweet boy,

160.play: And I with tears do wash the blood away.

161.play: Keep thou the napkin, and go boast of this:

162.play: And if thou tell'st the heavy story right,

163.play: Upon my soul, the hearers will shed tears;

164.play: Yea even my foes will shed fast-falling tears,

165.play: And say 'Alas, it was a piteous deed!'

166.play: There, take the crown, and, with the crown, my curse;

167.play: And in thy need such comfort come to thee

168.play: As now I reap at thy too cruel hand!

169.play: Hard-hearted Clifford, take me from the world:

170.play: My soul to heaven, my blood upon your heads!

171.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Had he been slaughter-man to all my kin,

172.play: I should not for my life but weep with him.

173.play: To see how inly sorrow gripes his soul.

174.play: QUEEN MARGARET: What, weeping-ripe, my Lord Northumberland?

175.play: Think but upon the wrong he did us all,

176.play: And that will quickly dry thy melting tears.

177.play: CLIFFORD: Here's for my oath, here's for my father's death.

178.play: QUEEN MARGARET: And here's to right our gentle-hearted king.

179.play: YORK: Open Thy gate of mercy, gracious God!

180.play: My soul flies through these wounds to seek out Thee.

181.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Off with his head, and set it on York gates;

182.play: So York may overlook the town of York.

1.play: EDWARD: I wonder how our princely father 'scaped,

2.play: Or whether he be 'scaped away or no

3.play: From Clifford's and Northumberland's pursuit:

4.play: Had he been ta'en, we should have heard the news;

5.play: Had he been slain, we should have heard the news;

6.play: Or had he 'scaped, methinks we should have heard

7.play: The happy tidings of his good escape.

8.play: How fares my brother? why is he so sad?

9.play: RICHARD: I cannot joy, until I be resolved

10.play: Where our right valiant father is become.

11.play: I saw him in the battle range about;

12.play: And watch'd him how he singled Clifford forth.

13.play: Methought he bore him in the thickest troop

14.play: As doth a lion in a herd of neat;

15.play: Or as a bear, encompass'd round with dogs,

16.play: Who having pinch'd a few and made them cry,

17.play: The rest stand all aloof, and bark at him.

18.play: So fared our father with his enemies;

19.play: So fled his enemies my warlike father:

20.play: Methinks, 'tis prize enough to be his son.

21.play: See how the morning opes her golden gates,

22.play: And takes her farewell of the glorious sun!

23.play: How well resembles it the prime of youth,

24.play: Trimm'd like a younker prancing to his love!

25.play: EDWARD: Dazzle mine eyes, or do I see three suns?

26.play: RICHARD: Three glorious suns, each one a perfect sun;

27.play: Not separated with the racking clouds,

28.play: But sever'd in a pale clear-shining sky.

29.play: See, see! they join, embrace, and seem to kiss,

30.play: As if they vow'd some league inviolable:

31.play: Now are they but one lamp, one light, one sun.

32.play: In this the heaven figures some event.

33.play: EDWARD: 'Tis wondrous strange, the like yet never heard of.

34.play: I think it cites us, brother, to the field,

35.play: That we, the sons of brave Plantagenet,

36.play: Each one already blazing by our meeds,

37.play: Should notwithstanding join our lights together

38.play: And over-shine the earth as this the world.

39.play: Whate'er it bodes, henceforward will I bear

40.play: Upon my target three fair-shining suns.

41.play: RICHARD: Nay, bear three daughters: by your leave I speak it,

42.play: You love the breeder better than the male.

43.play: But what art thou, whose heavy looks foretell

44.play: Some dreadful story hanging on thy tongue?

45.play: EDWARD: O, speak no more, for I have heard too much.

46.play: RICHARD: Say how he died, for I will hear it all.

47.play: EDWARD: Sweet Duke of York, our prop to lean upon,

48.play: Now thou art gone, we have no staff, no stay.

49.play: O Clifford, boisterous Clifford! thou hast slain

50.play: The flower of Europe for his chivalry;

51.play: And treacherously hast thou vanquish'd him,

52.play: For hand to hand he would have vanquish'd thee.

53.play: Now my soul's palace is become a prison:

54.play: Ah, would she break from hence, that this my body

55.play: Might in the ground be closed up in rest!

56.play: For never henceforth shall I joy again,

57.play: Never, O never shall I see more joy!

58.play: RICHARD: I cannot weep; for all my body's moisture

59.play: Scarce serves to quench my furnace-burning heart:

60.play: Nor can my tongue unload my heart's great burthen;

61.play: For selfsame wind that I should speak withal

62.play: Is kindling coals that fires all my breast,

63.play: And burns me up with flames that tears would quench.

64.play: To weep is to make less the depth of grief:

65.play: Tears then for babes; blows and revenge for me

66.play: Richard, I bear thy name; I'll venge thy death,

67.play: Or die renowned by attempting it.

68.play: EDWARD: His name that valiant duke hath left with thee;

69.play: His dukedom and his chair with me is left.

70.play: RICHARD: Nay, if thou be that princely eagle's bird,

71.play: Show thy descent by gazing 'gainst the sun:

72.play: For chair and dukedom, throne and kingdom say;

73.play: Either that is thine, or else thou wert not his.

74.play: WARWICK: How now, fair lords! What fare? what news abroad?

75.play: RICHARD: Great Lord of Warwick, if we should recount

76.play: Our baleful news, and at each word's deliverance

77.play: Stab poniards in our flesh till all were told,

78.play: The words would add more anguish than the wounds.

79.play: O valiant lord, the Duke of York is slain!

80.play: EDWARD: O Warwick, Warwick! that Plantagenet,

81.play: Which held three dearly as his soul's redemption,

82.play: Is by the stern Lord Clifford done to death.

83.play: WARWICK: Ten days ago I drown'd these news in tears;

84.play: And now, to add more measure to your woes,

85.play: I come to tell you things sith then befall'n.

86.play: After the bloody fray at Wakefield fought,

87.play: Where your brave father breathed his latest gasp,

88.play: Tidings, as swiftly as the posts could run,

89.play: Were brought me of your loss and his depart.

90.play: I, then in London keeper of the king,

91.play: Muster'd my soldiers, gather'd flocks of friends,

92.play: And very well appointed, as I thought,

93.play: March'd toward Saint Alban's to intercept the queen,

94.play: Bearing the king in my behalf along;

95.play: For by my scouts I was advertised

96.play: That she was coming with a full intent

97.play: To dash our late decree in parliament

98.play: Touching King Henry's oath and your succession.

99.play: Short tale to make, we at Saint Alban's met

100.play: Our battles join'd, and both sides fiercely fought:

101.play: But whether 'twas the coldness of the king,

102.play: Who look'd full gently on his warlike queen,

103.play: That robb'd my soldiers of their heated spleen;

104.play: Or whether 'twas report of her success;

105.play: Or more than common fear of Clifford's rigour,

106.play: Who thunders to his captives blood and death,

107.play: I cannot judge: but to conclude with truth,

108.play: Their weapons like to lightning came and went;

109.play: Our soldiers', like the night-owl's lazy flight,

110.play: Or like an idle thresher with a flail,

111.play: Fell gently down, as if they struck their friends.

112.play: I cheer'd them up with justice of our cause,

113.play: With promise of high pay and great rewards:

114.play: But all in vain; they had no heart to fight,

115.play: And we in them no hope to win the day;

116.play: So that we fled; the king unto the queen;

117.play: Lord George your brother, Norfolk and myself,

118.play: In haste, post-haste, are come to join with you:

119.play: For in the marches here we heard you were,

120.play: Making another head to fight again.

121.play: EDWARD: Where is the Duke of Norfolk, gentle Warwick?

122.play: And when came George from Burgundy to England?

123.play: WARWICK: Some six miles off the duke is with the soldiers;

124.play: And for your brother, he was lately sent

125.play: From your kind aunt, Duchess of Burgundy,

126.play: With aid of soldiers to this needful war.

127.play: RICHARD: 'Twas odds, belike, when valiant Warwick fled:

128.play: Oft have I heard his praises in pursuit,

129.play: But ne'er till now his scandal of retire.

130.play: WARWICK: Nor now my scandal, Richard, dost thou hear;

131.play: For thou shalt know this strong right hand of mine

132.play: Can pluck the diadem from faint Henry's head,

133.play: And wring the awful sceptre from his fist,

134.play: Were he as famous and as bold in war

135.play: As he is famed for mildness, peace, and prayer.

136.play: RICHARD: I know it well, Lord Warwick; blame me not:

137.play: 'Tis love I bear thy glories makes me speak.

138.play: But in this troublous time what's to be done?

139.play: Shall we go throw away our coats of steel,

140.play: And wrap our bodies in black mourning gowns,

141.play: Numbering our Ave-Maries with our beads?

142.play: Or shall we on the helmets of our foes

143.play: Tell our devotion with revengeful arms?

144.play: If for the last, say ay, and to it, lords.

145.play: WARWICK: Why, therefore Warwick came to seek you out;

146.play: And therefore comes my brother Montague.

147.play: Attend me, lords. The proud insulting queen,

148.play: With Clifford and the haught Northumberland,

149.play: And of their feather many more proud birds,

150.play: Have wrought the easy-melting king like wax.

151.play: He swore consent to your succession,

152.play: His oath enrolled in the parliament;

153.play: And now to London all the crew are gone,

154.play: To frustrate both his oath and what beside

155.play: May make against the house of Lancaster.

156.play: Their power, I think, is thirty thousand strong:

157.play: Now, if the help of Norfolk and myself,

158.play: With all the friends that thou, brave Earl of March,

159.play: Amongst the loving Welshmen canst procure,

160.play: Will but amount to five and twenty thousand,

161.play: Why, Via! to London will we march amain,

162.play: And once again bestride our foaming steeds,

163.play: And once again cry 'Charge upon our foes!'

164.play: But never once again turn back and fly.

165.play: RICHARD: Ay, now methinks I hear great Warwick speak:

166.play: Ne'er may he live to see a sunshine day,

167.play: That cries 'Retire,' if Warwick bid him stay.

168.play: EDWARD: Lord Warwick, on thy shoulder will I lean;

169.play: And when thou fail'st--as God forbid the hour!--

170.play: Must Edward fall, which peril heaven forfend!

171.play: WARWICK: No longer Earl of March, but Duke of York:

172.play: The next degree is England's royal throne;

173.play: For King of England shalt thou be proclaim'd

174.play: In every borough as we pass along;

175.play: And he that throws not up his cap for joy

176.play: Shall for the fault make forfeit of his head.

178.play: Stay we no longer, dreaming of renown,

179.play: But sound the trumpets, and about our task.

180.play: RICHARD: Then, Clifford, were thy heart as hard as steel,

181.play: As thou hast shown it flinty by thy deeds,

182.play: I come to pierce it, or to give thee mine.

183.play: EDWARD: Then strike up drums: God and Saint George for us!

185.play: Why then it sorts, brave warriors, let's away.

1.play: Flourish. Enter KING HENRY VI, QUEEN MARGARET,  PRINCE EDWARD, CLIFFORD, and NORTHUMBERLAND, with drum and trumpets

2.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Welcome, my lord, to this brave town of York.

3.play: Yonder's the head of that arch-enemy

4.play: That sought to be encompass'd with your crown:

5.play: Doth not the object cheer your heart, my lord?

6.play: KING HENRY VI: Ay, as the rocks cheer them that fear their wreck:

7.play: To see this sight, it irks my very soul.

8.play: Withhold revenge, dear God! 'tis not my fault,

9.play: Nor wittingly have I infringed my vow.

10.play: CLIFFORD: My gracious liege, this too much lenity

11.play: And harmful pity must be laid aside.

12.play: To whom do lions cast their gentle looks?

13.play: Not to the beast that would usurp their den.

14.play: Whose hand is that the forest bear doth lick?

15.play: Not his that spoils her young before her face.

16.play: Who 'scapes the lurking serpent's mortal sting?

17.play: Not he that sets his foot upon her back.

18.play: The smallest worm will turn being trodden on,

19.play: And doves will peck in safeguard of their brood.

20.play: Ambitious York doth level at thy crown,

21.play: Thou smiling while he knit his angry brows:

22.play: He, but a duke, would have his son a king,

23.play: And raise his issue, like a loving sire;

24.play: Thou, being a king, blest with a goodly son,

25.play: Didst yield consent to disinherit him,

26.play: Which argued thee a most unloving father.

27.play: Unreasonable creatures feed their young;

28.play: And though man's face be fearful to their eyes,

29.play: Yet, in protection of their tender ones,

30.play: Who hath not seen them, even with those wings

31.play: Which sometime they have used with fearful flight,

32.play: Make war with him that climb'd unto their nest,

33.play: Offer their own lives in their young's defence?

34.play: For shame, my liege, make them your precedent!

35.play: Were it not pity that this goodly boy

36.play: Should lose his birthright by his father's fault,

37.play: And long hereafter say unto his child,

38.play: 'What my great-grandfather and his grandsire got

39.play: My careless father fondly gave away'?

40.play: Ah, what a shame were this! Look on the boy;

41.play: And let his manly face, which promiseth

42.play: Successful fortune, steel thy melting heart

43.play: To hold thine own and leave thine own with him.

44.play: KING HENRY VI: Full well hath Clifford play'd the orator,

45.play: Inferring arguments of mighty force.

46.play: But, Clifford, tell me, didst thou never hear

47.play: That things ill-got had ever bad success?

48.play: And happy always was it for that son

49.play: Whose father for his hoarding went to hell?

50.play: I'll leave my son my virtuous deeds behind;

51.play: And would my father had left me no more!

52.play: For all the rest is held at such a rate

53.play: As brings a thousand-fold more care to keep

54.play: Than in possession and jot of pleasure.

55.play: Ah, cousin York! would thy best friends did know

56.play: How it doth grieve me that thy head is here!

57.play: QUEEN MARGARET: My lord, cheer up your spirits: our foes are nigh,

58.play: And this soft courage makes your followers faint.

59.play: You promised knighthood to our forward son:

60.play: Unsheathe your sword, and dub him presently.

62.play: KING HENRY VI: Edward Plantagenet, arise a knight;

63.play: And learn this lesson, draw thy sword in right.

64.play: PRINCE: My gracious father, by your kingly leave,

65.play: I'll draw it as apparent to the crown,

66.play: And in that quarrel use it to the death.

67.play: CLIFFORD: Why, that is spoken like a toward prince.

68.play: I would your highness would depart the field:

69.play: The queen hath best success when you are absent.

70.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Ay, good my lord, and leave us to our fortune.

71.play: KING HENRY VI: Why, that's my fortune too; therefore I'll stay.

72.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: Be it with resolution then to fight.

73.play: PRINCE EDWARD: My royal father, cheer these noble lords

74.play: And hearten those that fight in your defence:

75.play: Unsheathe your sword, good father; cry 'Saint George!'

76.play: EDWARD: Now, perjured Henry! wilt thou kneel for grace,

77.play: And set thy diadem upon my head;

78.play: Or bide the mortal fortune of the field?

79.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Go, rate thy minions, proud insulting boy!

80.play: Becomes it thee to be thus bold in terms

81.play: Before thy sovereign and thy lawful king?

82.play: EDWARD: I am his king, and he should bow his knee;

83.play: I was adopted heir by his consent:

84.play: Since when, his oath is broke; for, as I hear,

85.play: You, that are king, though he do wear the crown,

86.play: Have caused him, by new act of parliament,

87.play: To blot out me, and put his own son in.

89.play: Who should succeed the father but the son?

90.play: RICHARD: Are you there, butcher? O, I cannot speak!

91.play: CLIFFORD: Ay, crook-back, here I stand to answer thee,

92.play: Or any he the proudest of thy sort.

93.play: RICHARD: 'Twas you that kill'd young Rutland, was it not?

94.play: CLIFFORD: Ay, and old York, and yet not satisfied.

95.play: RICHARD: For God's sake, lords, give signal to the fight.

96.play: WARWICK: What say'st thou, Henry, wilt thou yield the crown?

97.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Why, how now, long-tongued Warwick! dare you speak?

98.play: When you and I met at Saint Alban's last,

99.play: Your legs did better service than your hands.

100.play: WARWICK: Then 'twas my turn to fly, and now 'tis thine.

101.play: CLIFFORD: You said so much before, and yet you fled.

102.play: WARWICK: 'Twas not your valour, Clifford, drove me thence.

103.play: NORTHUMBERLAND: No, nor your manhood that durst make you stay.

104.play: RICHARD: Northumberland, I hold thee reverently.

105.play: Break off the parley; for scarce I can refrain

106.play: The execution of my big-swoln heart

107.play: Upon that Clifford, that cruel child-killer.

108.play: CLIFFORD: I slew thy father, call'st thou him a child?

109.play: RICHARD: Ay, like a dastard and a treacherous coward,

110.play: As thou didst kill our tender brother Rutland;

111.play: But ere sunset I'll make thee curse the deed.

112.play: KING HENRY VI: Have done with words, my lords, and hear me speak.

113.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Defy them then, or else hold close thy lips.

114.play: KING HENRY VI: I prithee, give no limits to my tongue:

115.play: I am a king, and privileged to speak.

116.play: CLIFFORD: My liege, the wound that bred this meeting here

117.play: Cannot be cured by words; therefore be still.

118.play: RICHARD: Then, executioner, unsheathe thy sword:

119.play: By him that made us all, I am resolved

120.play: that Clifford's manhood lies upon his tongue.

121.play: EDWARD: Say, Henry, shall I have my right, or no?

122.play: A thousand men have broke their fasts to-day,

123.play: That ne'er shall dine unless thou yield the crown.

124.play: WARWICK: If thou deny, their blood upon thy head;

125.play: For York in justice puts his armour on.

126.play: PRINCE EDWARD: If that be right which Warwick says is right,

127.play: There is no wrong, but every thing is right.

128.play: RICHARD: Whoever got thee, there thy mother stands;

129.play: For, well I wot, thou hast thy mother's tongue.

130.play: QUEEN MARGARET: But thou art neither like thy sire nor dam;

131.play: But like a foul mis-shapen stigmatic,

132.play: Mark'd by the destinies to be avoided,

133.play: As venom toads, or lizards' dreadful stings.

134.play: RICHARD: Iron of Naples hid with English gilt,

135.play: Whose father bears the title of a king,--

136.play: As if a channel should be call'd the sea,--

137.play: Shamest thou not, knowing whence thou art extraught,

138.play: To let thy tongue detect thy base-born heart?

139.play: EDWARD: A wisp of straw were worth a thousand crowns,

140.play: To make this shameless callet know herself.

141.play: Helen of Greece was fairer far than thou,

142.play: Although thy husband may be Menelaus;

143.play: And ne'er was Agamemnon's brother wrong'd

144.play: By that false woman, as this king by thee.

145.play: His father revell'd in the heart of France,

146.play: And tamed the king, and made the dauphin stoop;

147.play: And had he match'd according to his state,

148.play: He might have kept that glory to this day;

149.play: But when he took a beggar to his bed,

150.play: And graced thy poor sire with his bridal-day,

151.play: Even then that sunshine brew'd a shower for him,

152.play: That wash'd his father's fortunes forth of France,

153.play: And heap'd sedition on his crown at home.

154.play: For what hath broach'd this tumult but thy pride?

155.play: Hadst thou been meek, our title still had slept;

156.play: And we, in pity of the gentle king,

157.play: Had slipp'd our claim until another age.

158.play: GEORGE: But when we saw our sunshine made thy spring,

159.play: And that thy summer bred us no increase,

160.play: We set the axe to thy usurping root;

161.play: And though the edge hath something hit ourselves,

162.play: Yet, know thou, since we have begun to strike,

163.play: We'll never leave till we have hewn thee down,

164.play: Or bathed thy growing with our heated bloods.

165.play: EDWARD: And, in this resolution, I defy thee;

167.play: Since thou deniest the gentle king to speak.

168.play: Sound trumpets! let our bloody colours wave!

169.play: And either victory, or else a grave.

171.play: EDWARD: No, wrangling woman, we'll no longer stay:

172.play: These words will cost ten thousand lives this day.

1.play: Alarum. Excursions. Enter WARWICK

2.play: WARWICK: Forspent with toil, as runners with a race,

3.play: I lay me down a little while to breathe;

4.play: For strokes received, and many blows repaid,

5.play: Have robb'd my strong-knit sinews of their strength,

6.play: And spite of spite needs must I rest awhile.

7.play: EDWARD: Smile, gentle heaven! or strike, ungentle death!

8.play: For this world frowns, and Edward's sun is clouded.

9.play: WARWICK: How now, my lord! what hap? what hope of good?

10.play: GEORGE: Our hap is loss, our hope but sad despair;

11.play: Our ranks are broke, and ruin follows us:

12.play: What counsel give you? whither shall we fly?

13.play: EDWARD: Bootless is flight, they follow us with wings;

14.play: And weak we are and cannot shun pursuit.

15.play: RICHARD: Ah, Warwick, why hast thou withdrawn thyself?

16.play: Thy brother's blood the thirsty earth hath drunk,

17.play: Broach'd with the steely point of Clifford's lance;

18.play: And in the very pangs of death he cried,

19.play: Like to a dismal clangour heard from far,

20.play: 'Warwick, revenge! brother, revenge my death!'

21.play: So, underneath the belly of their steeds,

22.play: That stain'd their fetlocks in his smoking blood,

23.play: The noble gentleman gave up the ghost.

24.play: WARWICK: Then let the earth be drunken with our blood:

25.play: I'll kill my horse, because I will not fly.

26.play: Why stand we like soft-hearted women here,

27.play: Wailing our losses, whiles the foe doth rage;

28.play: And look upon, as if the tragedy

29.play: Were play'd in jest by counterfeiting actors?

30.play: Here on my knee I vow to God above,

31.play: I'll never pause again, never stand still,

32.play: Till either death hath closed these eyes of mine

33.play: Or fortune given me measure of revenge.

34.play: EDWARD: O Warwick, I do bend my knee with thine;

35.play: And in this vow do chain my soul to thine!

36.play: And, ere my knee rise from the earth's cold face,

37.play: I throw my hands, mine eyes, my heart to thee,

38.play: Thou setter up and plucker down of kings,

39.play: Beseeching thee, if with they will it stands

40.play: That to my foes this body must be prey,

41.play: Yet that thy brazen gates of heaven may ope,

42.play: And give sweet passage to my sinful soul!

43.play: Now, lords, take leave until we meet again,

44.play: Where'er it be, in heaven or in earth.

45.play: RICHARD: Brother, give me thy hand; and, gentle Warwick,

46.play: Let me embrace thee in my weary arms:

47.play: I, that did never weep, now melt with woe

48.play: That winter should cut off our spring-time so.

49.play: WARWICK: Away, away! Once more, sweet lords farewell.

50.play: GEORGE: Yet let us all together to our troops,

51.play: And give them leave to fly that will not stay;

52.play: And call them pillars that will stand to us;

53.play: And, if we thrive, promise them such rewards

54.play: As victors wear at the Olympian games:

55.play: This may plant courage in their quailing breasts;

56.play: For yet is hope of life and victory.

57.play: Forslow no longer, make we hence amain.

1.play: RICHARD: Now, Clifford, I have singled thee alone:

2.play: Suppose this arm is for the Duke of York,

3.play: And this for Rutland; both bound to revenge,

4.play: Wert thou environ'd with a brazen wall.

5.play: CLIFFORD: Now, Richard, I am with thee here alone:

6.play: This is the hand that stabb'd thy father York;

7.play: And this the hand that slew thy brother Rutland;

8.play: And here's the heart that triumphs in their death

9.play: And cheers these hands that slew thy sire and brother

10.play: To execute the like upon thyself;

11.play: And so, have at thee!

12.play: RICHARD: Nay Warwick, single out some other chase;

13.play: For I myself will hunt this wolf to death.

1.play: Alarum. Enter KING HENRY VI alone

2.play: KING HENRY VI: This battle fares like to the morning's war,

3.play: When dying clouds contend with growing light,

4.play: What time the shepherd, blowing of his nails,

5.play: Can neither call it perfect day nor night.

6.play: Now sways it this way, like a mighty sea

7.play: Forced by the tide to combat with the wind;

8.play: Now sways it that way, like the selfsame sea

9.play: Forced to retire by fury of the wind:

10.play: Sometime the flood prevails, and then the wind;

11.play: Now one the better, then another best;

12.play: Both tugging to be victors, breast to breast,

13.play: Yet neither conqueror nor conquered:

14.play: So is the equal of this fell war.

15.play: Here on this molehill will I sit me down.

16.play: To whom God will, there be the victory!

17.play: For Margaret my queen, and Clifford too,

18.play: Have chid me from the battle; swearing both

19.play: They prosper best of all when I am thence.

20.play: Would I were dead! if God's good will were so;

21.play: For what is in this world but grief and woe?

22.play: O God! methinks it were a happy life,

23.play: To be no better than a homely swain;

24.play: To sit upon a hill, as I do now,

25.play: To carve out dials quaintly, point by point,

26.play: Thereby to see the minutes how they run,

27.play: How many make the hour full complete;

28.play: How many hours bring about the day;

29.play: How many days will finish up the year;

30.play: How many years a mortal man may live.

31.play: When this is known, then to divide the times:

32.play: So many hours must I tend my flock;

33.play: So many hours must I take my rest;

34.play: So many hours must I contemplate;

35.play: So many hours must I sport myself;

36.play: So many days my ewes have been with young;

37.play: So many weeks ere the poor fools will ean:

38.play: So many years ere I shall shear the fleece:

39.play: So minutes, hours, days, months, and years,

40.play: Pass'd over to the end they were created,

41.play: Would bring white hairs unto a quiet grave.

42.play: Ah, what a life were this! how sweet! how lovely!

43.play: Gives not the hawthorn-bush a sweeter shade

44.play: To shepherds looking on their silly sheep,

45.play: Than doth a rich embroider'd canopy

46.play: To kings that fear their subjects' treachery?

47.play: O, yes, it doth; a thousand-fold it doth.

48.play: And to conclude, the shepherd's homely curds,

49.play: His cold thin drink out of his leather bottle.

50.play: His wonted sleep under a fresh tree's shade,

51.play: All which secure and sweetly he enjoys,

52.play: Is far beyond a prince's delicates,

53.play: His viands sparkling in a golden cup,

54.play: His body couched in a curious bed,

55.play: When care, mistrust, and treason waits on him.

56.play: O piteous spectacle! O bloody times!

57.play: Whiles lions war and battle for their dens,

58.play: Poor harmless lambs abide their enmity.

59.play: Weep, wretched man, I'll aid thee tear for tear;

60.play: And let our hearts and eyes, like civil war,

61.play: Be blind with tears, and break o'ercharged with grief.

62.play: Woe above woe! grief more than common grief!

63.play: O that my death would stay these ruthful deeds!

64.play: O pity, pity, gentle heaven, pity!

65.play: The red rose and the white are on his face,

66.play: The fatal colours of our striving houses:

67.play: The one his purple blood right well resembles;

68.play: The other his pale cheeks, methinks, presenteth:

69.play: Wither one rose, and let the other flourish;

70.play: If you contend, a thousand lives must wither.

71.play: How will the country for these woful chances

72.play: Misthink the king and not be satisfied!

73.play: Was ever king so grieved for subjects' woe?

74.play: Much is your sorrow; mine ten times so much.

77.play: KING HENRY VI: Sad-hearted men, much overgone with care,

78.play: Here sits a king more woful than you are.

79.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Fly, father, fly! for all your friends are fled,

80.play: And Warwick rages like a chafed bull:

81.play: Away! for death doth hold us in pursuit.

82.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Mount you, my lord; towards Berwick post amain:

83.play: Edward and Richard, like a brace of greyhounds

84.play: Having the fearful flying hare in sight,

85.play: With fiery eyes sparkling for very wrath,

86.play: And bloody steel grasp'd in their ireful hands,

87.play: Are at our backs; and therefore hence amain.

88.play: EXETER: Away! for vengeance comes along with them:

89.play: Nay, stay not to expostulate, make speed;

90.play: Or else come after: I'll away before.

91.play: KING HENRY VI: Nay, take me with thee, good sweet Exeter:

92.play: Not that I fear to stay, but love to go

93.play: Whither the queen intends. Forward; away!

1.play: CLIFFORD: Here burns my candle out; ay, here it dies,

2.play: Which, whiles it lasted, gave King Henry light.

3.play: O Lancaster, I fear thy overthrow

4.play: More than my body's parting with my soul!

5.play: My love and fear glued many friends to thee;

6.play: And, now I fall, thy tough commixture melts.

7.play: Impairing Henry, strengthening misproud York,

8.play: The common people swarm like summer flies;

9.play: And whither fly the gnats but to the sun?

10.play: And who shines now but Henry's enemies?

11.play: O Phoebus, hadst thou never given consent

12.play: That Phaethon should cheque thy fiery steeds,

13.play: Thy burning car never had scorch'd the earth!

14.play: And, Henry, hadst thou sway'd as kings should do,

15.play: Or as thy father and his father did,

16.play: Giving no ground unto the house of York,

17.play: They never then had sprung like summer flies;

18.play: I and ten thousand in this luckless realm

19.play: Had left no mourning widows for our death;

20.play: And thou this day hadst kept thy chair in peace.

21.play: For what doth cherish weeds but gentle air?

22.play: And what makes robbers bold but too much lenity?

23.play: Bootless are plaints, and cureless are my wounds;

24.play: No way to fly, nor strength to hold out flight:

25.play: The foe is merciless, and will not pity;

26.play: For at their hands I have deserved no pity.

27.play: The air hath got into my deadly wounds,

28.play: And much effuse of blood doth make me faint.

29.play: Come, York and Richard, Warwick and the rest;

30.play: I stabb'd your fathers' bosoms, split my breast.

31.play: EDWARD: Now breathe we, lords: good fortune bids us pause,

32.play: And smooth the frowns of war with peaceful looks.

33.play: Some troops pursue the bloody-minded queen,

34.play: That led calm Henry, though he were a king,

35.play: As doth a sail, fill'd with a fretting gust,

36.play: Command an argosy to stem the waves.

37.play: But think you, lords, that Clifford fled with them?

38.play: WARWICK: No, 'tis impossible he should escape,

39.play: For, though before his face I speak the words

40.play: Your brother Richard mark'd him for the grave:

41.play: And wheresoe'er he is, he's surely dead.

42.play: EDWARD: Whose soul is that which takes her heavy leave?

43.play: RICHARD: A deadly groan, like life and death's departing.

44.play: EDWARD: See who it is: and, now the battle's ended,

45.play: If friend or foe, let him be gently used.

46.play: RICHARD: Revoke that doom of mercy, for 'tis Clifford;

47.play: Who not contented that he lopp'd the branch

48.play: In hewing Rutland when his leaves put forth,

49.play: But set his murdering knife unto the root

50.play: From whence that tender spray did sweetly spring,

51.play: I mean our princely father, Duke of York.

52.play: WARWICK: From off the gates of York fetch down the head,

53.play: Your father's head, which Clifford placed there;

54.play: Instead whereof let this supply the room:

55.play: Measure for measure must be answered.

56.play: EDWARD: Bring forth that fatal screech-owl to our house,

57.play: That nothing sung but death to us and ours:

58.play: Now death shall stop his dismal threatening sound,

59.play: And his ill-boding tongue no more shall speak.

60.play: WARWICK: I think his understanding is bereft.

61.play: Speak, Clifford, dost thou know who speaks to thee?

62.play: Dark cloudy death o'ershades his beams of life,

63.play: And he nor sees nor hears us what we say.

64.play: RICHARD: O, would he did! and so perhaps he doth:

66.play: Because he would avoid such bitter taunts

67.play: Which in the time of death he gave our father.

68.play: GEORGE: If so thou think'st, vex him with eager words.

69.play: RICHARD: Clifford, ask mercy and obtain no grace.

70.play: EDWARD: Clifford, repent in bootless penitence.

71.play: WARWICK: Clifford, devise excuses for thy faults.

72.play: GEORGE: While we devise fell tortures for thy faults.

73.play: RICHARD: Thou didst love York, and I am son to York.

74.play: EDWARD: Thou pitied'st Rutland; I will pity thee.

75.play: GEORGE: Where's Captain Margaret, to fence you now?

76.play: WARWICK: They mock thee, Clifford: swear as thou wast wont.

77.play: RICHARD: What, not an oath? nay, then the world goes hard

78.play: When Clifford cannot spare his friends an oath.

79.play: I know by that he's dead; and, by my soul,

80.play: If this right hand would buy two hour's life,

81.play: That I in all despite might rail at him,

82.play: This hand should chop it off, and with the

84.play: Stifle the villain whose unstanched thirst

85.play: York and young Rutland could not satisfy.

86.play: WARWICK: Ay, but he's dead: off with the traitor's head,

87.play: And rear it in the place your father's stands.

88.play: And now to London with triumphant march,

89.play: There to be crowned England's royal king:

90.play: From whence shall Warwick cut the sea to France,

91.play: And ask the Lady Bona for thy queen:

92.play: So shalt thou sinew both these lands together;

93.play: And, having France thy friend, thou shalt not dread

94.play: The scatter'd foe that hopes to rise again;

95.play: For though they cannot greatly sting to hurt,

96.play: Yet look to have them buzz to offend thine ears.

97.play: First will I see the coronation;

98.play: And then to Brittany I'll cross the sea,

99.play: To effect this marriage, so it please my lord.

100.play: EDWARD: Even as thou wilt, sweet Warwick, let it be;

101.play: For in thy shoulder do I build my seat,

102.play: And never will I undertake the thing

103.play: Wherein thy counsel and consent is wanting.

104.play: Richard, I will create thee Duke of Gloucester,

105.play: And George, of Clarence: Warwick, as ourself,

106.play: Shall do and undo as him pleaseth best.

107.play: RICHARD: Let me be Duke of Clarence, George of Gloucester;

108.play: For Gloucester's dukedom is too ominous.

109.play: WARWICK: Tut, that's a foolish observation:

110.play: Richard, be Duke of Gloucester. Now to London,

111.play: To see these honours in possession.

1.play: Enter two Keepers, with cross-bows in their hands

2.play: Enter KING HENRY VI, disguised, with a prayerbook

3.play: KING HENRY VI: From Scotland am I stol'n, even of pure love,

4.play: To greet mine own land with my wishful sight.

5.play: No, Harry, Harry, 'tis no land of thine;

6.play: Thy place is fill'd, thy sceptre wrung from thee,

7.play: Thy balm wash'd off wherewith thou wast anointed:

8.play: No bending knee will call thee Caesar now,

9.play: No humble suitors press to speak for right,

10.play: No, not a man comes for redress of thee;

11.play: For how can I help them, and not myself?

12.play: Let me embrace thee, sour adversity,

13.play: For wise men say it is the wisest course.

14.play: My queen and son are gone to France for aid;

15.play: And, as I hear, the great commanding Warwick

16.play: Is thither gone, to crave the French king's sister

17.play: To wife for Edward: if this news be true,

18.play: Poor queen and son, your labour is but lost;

19.play: For Warwick is a subtle orator,

20.play: And Lewis a prince soon won with moving words.

21.play: By this account then Margaret may win him;

22.play: For she's a woman to be pitied much:

23.play: Her sighs will make a battery in his breast;

24.play: Her tears will pierce into a marble heart;

25.play: The tiger will be mild whiles she doth mourn;

26.play: And Nero will be tainted with remorse,

27.play: To hear and see her plaints, her brinish tears.

28.play: Ay, but she's come to beg, Warwick to give;

29.play: She, on his left side, craving aid for Henry,

30.play: He, on his right, asking a wife for Edward.

31.play: She weeps, and says her Henry is deposed;

32.play: He smiles, and says his Edward is install'd;

33.play: That she, poor wretch, for grief can speak no more;

34.play: Whiles Warwick tells his title, smooths the wrong,

35.play: Inferreth arguments of mighty strength,

36.play: And in conclusion wins the king from her,

37.play: With promise of his sister, and what else,

38.play: To strengthen and support King Edward's place.

39.play: O Margaret, thus 'twill be; and thou, poor soul,

40.play: Art then forsaken, as thou went'st forlorn!

41.play: More than I seem, and less than I was born to:

42.play: A man at least, for less I should not be;

43.play: And men may talk of kings, and why not I?

44.play: Why, so I am, in mind; and that's enough.

45.play: My crown is in my heart, not on my head;

46.play: Not decked with diamonds and Indian stones,

47.play: Nor to be seen: my crown is called content:

48.play: A crown it is that seldom kings enjoy.

49.play: But did you never swear, and break an oath?

50.play: Where did you dwell when I was King of England?

51.play: I was anointed king at nine months old;

52.play: My father and my grandfather were kings,

53.play: And you were sworn true subjects unto me:

54.play: And tell me, then, have you not broke your oaths?

55.play: Why, am I dead? do I not breathe a man?

56.play: Ah, simple men, you know not what you swear!

57.play: Look, as I blow this feather from my face,

58.play: And as the air blows it to me again,

59.play: Obeying with my wind when I do blow,

60.play: And yielding to another when it blows,

61.play: Commanded always by the greater gust;

62.play: Such is the lightness of you common men.

63.play: But do not break your oaths; for of that sin

64.play: My mild entreaty shall not make you guilty.

65.play: Go where you will, the king shall be commanded;

66.play: And be you kings, command, and I'll obey.

67.play: So would you be again to Henry,

68.play: If he were seated as King Edward is.

69.play: In God's name, lead; your king's name be obey'd:

70.play: And what God will, that let your king perform;

71.play: And what he will, I humbly yield unto.

1.play: Enter KING EDWARD IV, GLOUCESTER, CLARENCE, and LADY GREY

2.play: KING EDWARD IV: Brother of Gloucester, at Saint Alban's field

3.play: This lady's husband, Sir Richard Grey, was slain,

4.play: His lands then seized on by the conqueror:

5.play: Her suit is now to repossess those lands;

6.play: Which we in justice cannot well deny,

7.play: Because in quarrel of the house of York

8.play: The worthy gentleman did lose his life.

9.play: GLOUCESTER: Your highness shall do well to grant her suit;

10.play: It were dishonour to deny it her.

11.play: KING EDWARD IV: It were no less; but yet I'll make a pause.

12.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  Yea, is it so?

13.play: I see the lady hath a thing to grant,

14.play: Before the king will grant her humble suit.

15.play: CLARENCE: [Aside to GLOUCESTER]  He knows the game: how true

16.play: he keeps the wind!

17.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  Silence!

18.play: KING EDWARD IV: Widow, we will consider of your suit;

19.play: And come some other time to know our mind.

20.play: LADY GREY: Right gracious lord, I cannot brook delay:

21.play: May it please your highness to resolve me now;

22.play: And what your pleasure is, shall satisfy me.

23.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  Ay, widow? then I'll warrant

24.play: you all your lands,

25.play: An if what pleases him shall pleasure you.

26.play: Fight closer, or, good faith, you'll catch a blow.

27.play: CLARENCE: [Aside to GLOUCESTER]  I fear her not, unless she

28.play: chance to fall.

29.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE] God forbid that! for he'll

30.play: take vantages.

31.play: KING EDWARD IV: How many children hast thou, widow? tell me.

32.play: CLARENCE: [Aside to GLOUCESTER]  I think he means to beg a

33.play: child of her.

34.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  Nay, whip me then: he'll rather

35.play: give her two.

36.play: LADY GREY: Three, my most gracious lord.

37.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  You shall have four, if you'll

38.play: be ruled by him.

39.play: KING EDWARD IV: 'Twere pity they should lose their father's lands.

40.play: LADY GREY: Be pitiful, dread lord, and grant it then.

41.play: KING EDWARD IV: Lords, give us leave: I'll try this widow's wit.

42.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  Ay, good leave have you; for

43.play: you will have leave,

44.play: Till youth take leave and leave you to the crutch.

45.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now tell me, madam, do you love your children?

46.play: LADY GREY: Ay, full as dearly as I love myself.

47.play: KING EDWARD IV: And would you not do much to do them good?

48.play: LADY GREY: To do them good, I would sustain some harm.

49.play: KING EDWARD IV: Then get your husband's lands, to do them good.

50.play: LADY GREY: Therefore I came unto your majesty.

51.play: KING EDWARD IV: I'll tell you how these lands are to be got.

52.play: LADY GREY: So shall you bind me to your highness' service.

53.play: KING EDWARD IV: What service wilt thou do me, if I give them?

54.play: LADY GREY: What you command, that rests in me to do.

55.play: KING EDWARD IV: But you will take exceptions to my boon.

56.play: LADY GREY: No, gracious lord, except I cannot do it.

57.play: KING EDWARD IV: Ay, but thou canst do what I mean to ask.

58.play: LADY GREY: Why, then I will do what your grace commands.

59.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  He plies her hard; and much rain

61.play: CLARENCE: [Aside to GLOUCESTER]  As red as fire! nay, then

63.play: LADY GREY: Why stops my lord, shall I not hear my task?

64.play: KING EDWARD IV: An easy task; 'tis but to love a king.

65.play: LADY GREY: That's soon perform'd, because I am a subject.

66.play: KING EDWARD IV: Why, then, thy husband's lands I freely give thee.

67.play: LADY GREY: I take my leave with many thousand thanks.

68.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  The match is made; she seals it

70.play: KING EDWARD IV: But stay thee, 'tis the fruits of love I mean.

71.play: LADY GREY: The fruits of love I mean, my loving liege.

72.play: KING EDWARD IV: Ay, but, I fear me, in another sense.

73.play: What love, think'st thou, I sue so much to get?

74.play: LADY GREY: My love till death, my humble thanks, my prayers;

75.play: That love which virtue begs and virtue grants.

76.play: KING EDWARD IV: No, by my troth, I did not mean such love.

77.play: LADY GREY: Why, then you mean not as I thought you did.

78.play: KING EDWARD IV: But now you partly may perceive my mind.

79.play: LADY GREY: My mind will never grant what I perceive

80.play: Your highness aims at, if I aim aright.

81.play: KING EDWARD IV: To tell thee plain, I aim to lie with thee.

82.play: LADY GREY: To tell you plain, I had rather lie in prison.

83.play: KING EDWARD IV: Why, then thou shalt not have thy husband's lands.

84.play: LADY GREY: Why, then mine honesty shall be my dower;

85.play: For by that loss I will not purchase them.

86.play: KING EDWARD IV: Therein thou wrong'st thy children mightily.

87.play: LADY GREY: Herein your highness wrongs both them and me.

88.play: But, mighty lord, this merry inclination

89.play: Accords not with the sadness of my suit:

90.play: Please you dismiss me either with 'ay' or 'no.'

91.play: KING EDWARD IV: Ay, if thou wilt say 'ay' to my request;

92.play: No if thou dost say 'no' to my demand.

93.play: LADY GREY: Then, no, my lord. My suit is at an end.

94.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  The widow likes him not, she

96.play: CLARENCE: [Aside to GLOUCESTER]  He is the bluntest wooer in

98.play: KING EDWARD IV: [Aside]  Her looks do argue her replete with modesty;

99.play: Her words do show her wit incomparable;

101.play: One way or other, she is for a king;

102.play: And she shall be my love, or else my queen.--

103.play: Say that King Edward take thee for his queen?

104.play: LADY GREY: 'Tis better said than done, my gracious lord:

105.play: I am a subject fit to jest withal,

106.play: But far unfit to be a sovereign.

107.play: KING EDWARD IV: Sweet widow, by my state I swear to thee

108.play: I speak no more than what my soul intends;

109.play: And that is, to enjoy thee for my love.

110.play: LADY GREY: And that is more than I will yield unto:

111.play: I know I am too mean to be your queen,

112.play: And yet too good to be your concubine.

113.play: KING EDWARD IV: You cavil, widow: I did mean, my queen.

114.play: LADY GREY: 'Twill grieve your grace my sons should call you father.

115.play: KING EDWARD IV: No more than when my daughters call thee mother.

116.play: Thou art a widow, and thou hast some children;

117.play: And, by God's mother, I, being but a bachelor,

118.play: Have other some: why, 'tis a happy thing

119.play: To be the father unto many sons.

120.play: Answer no more, for thou shalt be my queen.

121.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside to CLARENCE]  The ghostly father now hath done

123.play: CLARENCE: [Aside to GLOUCESTER]  When he was made a shriver,

125.play: KING EDWARD IV: Brothers, you muse what chat we two have had.

126.play: GLOUCESTER: The widow likes it not, for she looks very sad.

127.play: KING EDWARD IV: You'll think it strange if I should marry her.

129.play: KING EDWARD IV: Why, Clarence, to myself.

130.play: GLOUCESTER: That would be ten days' wonder at the least.

131.play: CLARENCE: That's a day longer than a wonder lasts.

132.play: GLOUCESTER: By so much is the wonder in extremes.
