133.play: KING EDWARD IV: Well, jest on, brothers: I can tell you both

134.play: Her suit is granted for her husband's lands.

135.play: See that he be convey'd unto the Tower:

136.play: And go we, brothers, to the man that took him,

138.play: Widow, go you along. Lords, use her honourably.

139.play: GLOUCESTER: Ay, Edward will use women honourably.

140.play: Would he were wasted, marrow, bones and all,

141.play: That from his loins no hopeful branch may spring,

142.play: To cross me from the golden time I look for!

143.play: And yet, between my soul's desire and me--

145.play: Is Clarence, Henry, and his son young Edward,

146.play: And all the unlook'd for issue of their bodies,

147.play: To take their rooms, ere I can place myself:

148.play: A cold premeditation for my purpose!

149.play: Why, then, I do but dream on sovereignty;

150.play: Like one that stands upon a promontory,

151.play: And spies a far-off shore where he would tread,

152.play: Wishing his foot were equal with his eye,

153.play: And chides the sea that sunders him from thence,

154.play: Saying, he'll lade it dry to have his way:

155.play: So do I wish the crown, being so far off;

156.play: And so I chide the means that keeps me from it;

157.play: And so I say, I'll cut the causes off,

159.play: My eye's too quick, my heart o'erweens too much,

160.play: Unless my hand and strength could equal them.

161.play: Well, say there is no kingdom then for Richard;

162.play: What other pleasure can the world afford?

163.play: I'll make my heaven in a lady's lap,

164.play: And deck my body in gay ornaments,

165.play: And witch sweet ladies with my words and looks.

166.play: O miserable thought! and more unlikely

167.play: Than to accomplish twenty golden crowns!

168.play: Why, love forswore me in my  mother's womb:

169.play: And, for I should not deal in her soft laws,

170.play: She did corrupt frail nature with some bribe,

171.play: To shrink mine arm up like a wither'd shrub;

172.play: To make an envious mountain on my back,

173.play: Where sits deformity to mock my body;

174.play: To shape my legs of an unequal size;

175.play: To disproportion me in every part,

176.play: Like to a chaos, or an unlick'd bear-whelp

177.play: That carries no impression like the dam.

178.play: And am I then a man to be beloved?

179.play: O monstrous fault, to harbour such a thought!

180.play: Then, since this earth affords no joy to me,

181.play: But to command, to cheque, to o'erbear such

182.play: As are of better person than myself,

183.play: I'll make my heaven to dream upon the crown,

184.play: And, whiles I live, to account this world but hell,

185.play: Until my mis-shaped trunk that bears this head

186.play: Be round impaled with a glorious crown.

187.play: And yet I know not how to get the crown,

188.play: For many lives stand between me and home:

189.play: And I,--like one lost in a thorny wood,

190.play: That rends the thorns and is rent with the thorns,

191.play: Seeking a way and straying from the way;

192.play: Not knowing how to find the open air,

193.play: But toiling desperately to find it out,--

194.play: Torment myself to catch the English crown:

195.play: And from that torment I will free myself,

196.play: Or hew my way out with a bloody axe.

197.play: Why, I can smile, and murder whiles I smile,

198.play: And cry 'Content' to that which grieves my heart,

199.play: And wet my cheeks with artificial tears,

200.play: And frame my face to all occasions.

201.play: I'll drown more sailors than the mermaid shall;

202.play: I'll slay more gazers than the basilisk;

203.play: I'll play the orator as well as Nestor,

204.play: Deceive more slily than Ulysses could,

205.play: And, like a Sinon, take another Troy.

206.play: I can add colours to the chameleon,

207.play: Change shapes with Proteus for advantages,

208.play: And set the murderous Machiavel to school.

209.play: Can I do this, and cannot get a crown?

210.play: Tut, were it farther off, I'll pluck it down.

1.play: Flourish. Enter KING LEWIS XI, his sister BONA,  his Admiral, called BOURBON, PRINCE EDWARD, QUEEN  MARGARET, and OXFORD. KING LEWIS XI sits, and riseth up again

2.play: KING LEWIS XI: Fair Queen of England, worthy Margaret,

3.play: Sit down with us: it ill befits thy state

4.play: And birth, that thou shouldst stand while Lewis doth sit.

5.play: QUEEN MARGARET: No, mighty King of France: now Margaret

6.play: Must strike her sail and learn awhile to serve

7.play: Where kings command. I was, I must confess,

8.play: Great Albion's queen in former golden days:

9.play: But now mischance hath trod my title down,

10.play: And with dishonour laid me on the ground;

11.play: Where I must take like seat unto my fortune,

12.play: And to my humble seat conform myself.

13.play: KING LEWIS XI: Why, say, fair queen, whence springs this deep despair?

14.play: QUEEN MARGARET: From such a cause as fills mine eyes with tears

15.play: And stops my tongue, while heart is drown'd in cares.

16.play: KING LEWIS XI: Whate'er it be, be thou still like thyself,

17.play: And sit thee by our side:

18.play: Yield not thy neck

19.play: To fortune's yoke, but let thy dauntless mind

20.play: Still ride in triumph over all mischance.

21.play: Be plain, Queen Margaret, and tell thy grief;

22.play: It shall be eased, if France can yield relief.

23.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Those gracious words revive my drooping thoughts

24.play: And give my tongue-tied sorrows leave to speak.

25.play: Now, therefore, be it known to noble Lewis,

26.play: That Henry, sole possessor of my love,

27.play: Is of a king become a banish'd man,

28.play: And forced to live in Scotland a forlorn;

29.play: While proud ambitious Edward Duke of York

30.play: Usurps the regal title and the seat

31.play: Of England's true-anointed lawful king.

32.play: This is the cause that I, poor Margaret,

33.play: With this my son, Prince Edward, Henry's heir,

34.play: Am come to crave thy just and lawful aid;

35.play: And if thou fail us, all our hope is done:

36.play: Scotland hath will to help, but cannot help;

37.play: Our people and our peers are both misled,

38.play: Our treasures seized, our soldiers put to flight,

39.play: And, as thou seest, ourselves in heavy plight.

40.play: KING LEWIS XI: Renowned queen, with patience calm the storm,

41.play: While we bethink a means to break it off.

42.play: QUEEN MARGARET: The more we stay, the stronger grows our foe.

43.play: KING LEWIS XI: The more I stay, the more I'll succor thee.

44.play: QUEEN MARGARET: O, but impatience waiteth on true sorrow.

45.play: And see where comes the breeder of my sorrow!

46.play: KING LEWIS XI: What's he approacheth boldly to our presence?

47.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Our Earl of Warwick, Edward's greatest friend.

48.play: KING LEWIS XI: Welcome, brave Warwick! What brings thee to France?

49.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Ay, now begins a second storm to rise;

50.play: For this is he that moves both wind and tide.

51.play: WARWICK: From worthy Edward, King of Albion,

52.play: My lord and sovereign, and thy vowed friend,

53.play: I come, in kindness and unfeigned love,

54.play: First, to do greetings to thy royal person;

55.play: And then to crave a league of amity;

56.play: And lastly, to confirm that amity

57.play: With a nuptial knot, if thou vouchsafe to grant

58.play: That virtuous Lady Bona, thy fair sister,

59.play: To England's king in lawful marriage.

60.play: QUEEN MARGARET: [Aside]  If that go forward, Henry's hope is done.

61.play: WARWICK: [To BONA]  And, gracious madam, in our king's behalf,

62.play: I am commanded, with your leave and favour,

63.play: Humbly to kiss your hand, and with my tongue

64.play: To tell the passion of my sovereign's heart;

65.play: Where fame, late entering at his heedful ears,

66.play: Hath placed thy beauty's image and thy virtue.

67.play: QUEEN MARGARET: King Lewis and Lady Bona, hear me speak,

68.play: Before you answer Warwick. His demand

69.play: Springs not from Edward's well-meant honest love,

70.play: But from deceit bred by necessity;

71.play: For how can tyrants safely govern home,

72.play: Unless abroad they purchase great alliance?

73.play: To prove him tyrant this reason may suffice,

74.play: That Henry liveth still: but were he dead,

75.play: Yet here Prince Edward stands, King Henry's son.

76.play: Look, therefore, Lewis, that by this league and marriage

77.play: Thou draw not on thy danger and dishonour;

78.play: For though usurpers sway the rule awhile,

79.play: Yet heavens are just, and time suppresseth wrongs.

81.play: PRINCE EDWARD: And why not queen?

82.play: WARWICK: Because thy father Henry did usurp;

83.play: And thou no more are prince than she is queen.

84.play: OXFORD: Then Warwick disannuls great John of Gaunt,

85.play: Which did subdue the greatest part of Spain;

86.play: And, after John of Gaunt, Henry the Fourth,

87.play: Whose wisdom was a mirror to the wisest;

88.play: And, after that wise prince, Henry the Fifth,

89.play: Who by his prowess conquered all France:

90.play: From these our Henry lineally descends.

91.play: WARWICK: Oxford, how haps it, in this smooth discourse,

92.play: You told not how Henry the Sixth hath lost

93.play: All that which Henry Fifth had gotten?

94.play: Methinks these peers of France should smile at that.

95.play: But for the rest, you tell a pedigree

96.play: Of threescore and two years; a silly time

97.play: To make prescription for a kingdom's worth.

98.play: OXFORD: Why, Warwick, canst thou speak against thy liege,

99.play: Whom thou obeyed'st thirty and six years,

100.play: And not bewray thy treason with a blush?

101.play: WARWICK: Can Oxford, that did ever fence the right,

102.play: Now buckler falsehood with a pedigree?

103.play: For shame! leave Henry, and call Edward king.

104.play: OXFORD: Call him my king by whose injurious doom

105.play: My elder brother, the Lord Aubrey Vere,

106.play: Was done to death? and more than so, my father,

107.play: Even in the downfall of his mellow'd years,

108.play: When nature brought him to the door of death?

109.play: No, Warwick, no; while life upholds this arm,

110.play: This arm upholds the house of Lancaster.

111.play: WARWICK: And I the house of York.

112.play: KING LEWIS XI: Queen Margaret, Prince Edward, and Oxford,

113.play: Vouchsafe, at our request, to stand aside,

114.play: While I use further conference with Warwick.

115.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Heavens grant that Warwick's words bewitch him not!

116.play: KING LEWIS XI: Now Warwick, tell me, even upon thy conscience,

117.play: Is Edward your true king? for I were loath

118.play: To link with him that were not lawful chosen.

119.play: WARWICK: Thereon I pawn my credit and mine honour.

120.play: KING LEWIS XI: But is he gracious in the people's eye?

121.play: WARWICK: The more that Henry was unfortunate.

122.play: KING LEWIS XI: Then further, all dissembling set aside,

123.play: Tell me for truth the measure of his love

126.play: As may beseem a monarch like himself.

127.play: Myself have often heard him say and swear

128.play: That this his love was an eternal plant,

129.play: Whereof the root was fix'd in virtue's ground,

130.play: The leaves and fruit maintain'd with beauty's sun,

131.play: Exempt from envy, but not from disdain,

132.play: Unless the Lady Bona quit his pain.

133.play: KING LEWIS XI: Now, sister, let us hear your firm resolve.

134.play: BONA: Your grant, or your denial, shall be mine:

135.play: Yet I confess that often ere this day,

136.play: When I have heard your king's desert recounted,

137.play: Mine ear hath tempted judgment to desire.

138.play: KING LEWIS XI: Then, Warwick, thus: our sister shall be Edward's;

139.play: And now forthwith shall articles be drawn

140.play: Touching the jointure that your king must make,

141.play: Which with her dowry shall be counterpoised.

142.play: Draw near, Queen Margaret, and be a witness

143.play: That Bona shall be wife to the English king.

144.play: PRINCE EDWARD: To Edward, but not to the English king.

145.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Deceitful Warwick! it was thy device

146.play: By this alliance to make void my suit:

147.play: Before thy coming Lewis was Henry's friend.

148.play: KING LEWIS XI: And still is friend to him and Margaret:

149.play: But if your title to the crown be weak,

150.play: As may appear by Edward's good success,

151.play: Then 'tis but reason that I be released

152.play: From giving aid which late I promised.

153.play: Yet shall you have all kindness at my hand

154.play: That your estate requires and mine can yield.

155.play: WARWICK: Henry now lives in Scotland at his ease,

156.play: Where having nothing, nothing can he lose.

157.play: And as for you yourself, our quondam queen,

158.play: You have a father able to maintain you;

159.play: And better 'twere you troubled him than France.

160.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Peace, impudent and shameless Warwick, peace,

161.play: Proud setter up and puller down of kings!

162.play: I will not hence, till, with my talk and tears,

163.play: Both full of truth, I make King Lewis behold

164.play: Thy sly conveyance and thy lord's false love;

165.play: For both of you are birds of selfsame feather.

166.play: KING LEWIS XI: Warwick, this is some post to us or thee.

167.play: OXFORD: I like it well that our fair queen and mistress

168.play: Smiles at her news, while Warwick frowns at his.

169.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Nay, mark how Lewis stamps, as he were nettled:

170.play: I hope all's for the best.

171.play: KING LEWIS XI: Warwick, what are thy news? and yours, fair queen?

172.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Mine, such as fill my heart with unhoped joys.

173.play: WARWICK: Mine, full of sorrow and heart's discontent.

174.play: KING LEWIS XI: What! has your king married the Lady Grey!

175.play: And now, to soothe your forgery and his,

176.play: Sends me a paper to persuade me patience?

177.play: Is this the alliance that he seeks with France?

178.play: Dare he presume to scorn us in this manner?

179.play: QUEEN MARGARET: I told your majesty as much before:

180.play: This proveth Edward's love and Warwick's honesty.

181.play: WARWICK: King Lewis, I here protest, in sight of heaven,

182.play: And by the hope I have of heavenly bliss,

183.play: That I am clear from this misdeed of Edward's,

184.play: No more my king, for he dishonours me,

185.play: But most himself, if he could see his shame.

186.play: Did I forget that by the house of York

187.play: My father came untimely to his death?

188.play: Did I let pass the abuse done to my niece?

189.play: Did I impale him with the regal crown?

190.play: Did I put Henry from his native right?

191.play: And am I guerdon'd at the last with shame?

192.play: Shame on himself! for my desert is honour:

193.play: And to repair my honour lost for him,

194.play: I here renounce him and return to Henry.

195.play: My noble queen, let former grudges pass,

196.play: And henceforth I am thy true servitor:

197.play: I will revenge his wrong to Lady Bona,

198.play: And replant Henry in his former state.

199.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Warwick, these words have turn'd my hate to love;

200.play: And I forgive and quite forget old faults,

201.play: And joy that thou becomest King Henry's friend.

202.play: WARWICK: So much his friend, ay, his unfeigned friend,

203.play: That, if King Lewis vouchsafe to furnish us

204.play: With some few bands of chosen soldiers,

205.play: I'll undertake to land them on our coast

206.play: And force the tyrant from his seat by war.

207.play: 'Tis not his new-made bride shall succor him:

208.play: And as for Clarence, as my letters tell me,

209.play: He's very likely now to fall from him,

210.play: For matching more for wanton lust than honour,

211.play: Or than for strength and safety of our country.

212.play: BONA: Dear brother, how shall Bona be revenged

213.play: But by thy help to this distressed queen?

214.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Renowned prince, how shall poor Henry live,

215.play: Unless thou rescue him from foul despair?

216.play: BONA: My quarrel and this English queen's are one.

217.play: WARWICK: And mine, fair lady Bona, joins with yours.

218.play: KING LEWIS XI: And mine with hers, and thine, and Margaret's.

219.play: Therefore at last I firmly am resolved

221.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Let me give humble thanks for all at once.

222.play: KING LEWIS XI: Then, England's messenger, return in post,

223.play: And tell false Edward, thy supposed king,

224.play: That Lewis of France is sending over masquers

225.play: To revel it with him and his new bride:

226.play: Thou seest what's past, go fear thy king withal.

227.play: BONA: Tell him, in hope he'll prove a widower shortly,

228.play: I'll wear the willow garland for his sake.

229.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Tell him, my mourning weeds are laid aside,

230.play: And I am ready to put armour on.

231.play: WARWICK: Tell him from me that he hath done me wrong,

232.play: And therefore I'll uncrown him ere't be long.

235.play: Thou and Oxford, with five thousand men,

236.play: Shall cross the seas, and bid false Edward battle;

237.play: And, as occasion serves, this noble queen

238.play: And prince shall follow with a fresh supply.

239.play: Yet, ere thou go, but answer me one doubt,

240.play: What pledge have we of thy firm loyalty?

241.play: WARWICK: This shall assure my constant loyalty,

242.play: That if our queen and this young prince agree,

243.play: I'll join mine eldest daughter and my joy

244.play: To him forthwith in holy wedlock bands.

245.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Yes, I agree, and thank you for your motion.

246.play: Son Edward, she is fair and virtuous,

247.play: Therefore delay not, give thy hand to Warwick;

248.play: And, with thy hand, thy faith irrevocable,

249.play: That only Warwick's daughter shall be thine.

250.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Yes, I accept her, for she well deserves it;

251.play: And here, to pledge my vow, I give my hand.

252.play: KING LEWIS XI: Why stay we now? These soldiers shall be levied,

253.play: And thou, Lord Bourbon, our high admiral,

254.play: Shalt waft them over with our royal fleet.

255.play: I long till Edward fall by war's mischance,

256.play: For mocking marriage with a dame of France.

257.play: WARWICK: I came from Edward as ambassador,

258.play: But I return his sworn and mortal foe:

259.play: Matter of marriage was the charge he gave me,

260.play: But dreadful war shall answer his demand.

261.play: Had he none else to make a stale but me?

262.play: Then none but I shall turn his jest to sorrow.

263.play: I was the chief that raised him to the crown,

264.play: And I'll be chief to bring him down again:

265.play: Not that I pity Henry's misery,

266.play: But seek revenge on Edward's mockery.

1.play: Enter GLOUCESTER, CLARENCE, SOMERSET, and MONTAGUE

2.play: GLOUCESTER: Now tell me, brother Clarence, what think you

3.play: Of this new marriage with the Lady Grey?

4.play: Hath not our brother made a worthy choice?

5.play: CLARENCE: Alas, you know, 'tis far from hence to France;

6.play: How could he stay till Warwick made return?

7.play: SOMERSET: My lords, forbear this talk; here comes the king.

8.play: GLOUCESTER: And his well-chosen bride.

9.play: CLARENCE: I mind to tell him plainly what I think.

10.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now, brother of Clarence, how like you our choice,

11.play: That you stand pensive, as half malcontent?

12.play: CLARENCE: As well as Lewis of France, or the Earl of Warwick,

13.play: Which are so weak of courage and in judgment

14.play: That they'll take no offence at our abuse.

15.play: KING EDWARD IV: Suppose they take offence without a cause,

16.play: They are but Lewis and Warwick: I am Edward,

17.play: Your king and Warwick's, and must have my will.

18.play: GLOUCESTER: And shall have your will, because our king:

19.play: Yet hasty marriage seldom proveth well.

20.play: KING EDWARD IV: Yea, brother Richard, are you offended too?

21.play: GLOUCESTER: Not I:

22.play: No, God forbid that I should wish them sever'd

23.play: Whom God hath join'd together; ay, and 'twere pity

24.play: To sunder them that yoke so well together.

25.play: KING EDWARD IV: Setting your scorns and your mislike aside,

26.play: Tell me some reason why the Lady Grey

27.play: Should not become my wife and England's queen.

28.play: And you too, Somerset and Montague,

29.play: Speak freely what you think.

30.play: CLARENCE: Then this is mine opinion: that King Lewis

31.play: Becomes your enemy, for mocking him

32.play: About the marriage of the Lady Bona.

33.play: GLOUCESTER: And Warwick, doing what you gave in charge,

34.play: Is now dishonoured by this new marriage.

35.play: KING EDWARD IV: What if both Lewis and Warwick be appeased

36.play: By such invention as I can devise?

37.play: MONTAGUE: Yet, to have join'd with France in such alliance

38.play: Would more have strengthen'd this our commonwealth

39.play: 'Gainst foreign storms than any home-bred marriage.

40.play: HASTINGS: Why, knows not Montague that of itself

41.play: England is safe, if true within itself?

42.play: MONTAGUE: But the safer when 'tis back'd with France.

43.play: HASTINGS: 'Tis better using France than trusting France:

44.play: Let us be back'd with God and with the seas

45.play: Which He hath given for fence impregnable,

46.play: And with their helps only defend ourselves;

47.play: In them and in ourselves our safety lies.

48.play: CLARENCE: For this one speech Lord Hastings well deserves

49.play: To have the heir of the Lord Hungerford.

50.play: KING EDWARD IV: Ay, what of that? it was my will and grant;

51.play: And for this once my will shall stand for law.

52.play: GLOUCESTER: And yet methinks your grace hath not done well,

53.play: To give the heir and daughter of Lord Scales

54.play: Unto the brother of your loving bride;

55.play: She better would have fitted me or Clarence:

56.play: But in your bride you bury brotherhood.

57.play: CLARENCE: Or else you would not have bestow'd the heir

58.play: Of the Lord Bonville on your new wife's son,

59.play: And leave your brothers to go speed elsewhere.

60.play: KING EDWARD IV: Alas, poor Clarence! is it for a wife

61.play: That thou art malcontent? I will provide thee.

62.play: CLARENCE: In choosing for yourself, you show'd your judgment,

63.play: Which being shallow, you give me leave

64.play: To play the broker in mine own behalf;

65.play: And to that end I shortly mind to leave you.

66.play: KING EDWARD IV: Leave me, or tarry, Edward will be king,

67.play: And not be tied unto his brother's will.

68.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: My lords, before it pleased his majesty

69.play: To raise my state to title of a queen,

70.play: Do me but right, and you must all confess

71.play: That I was not ignoble of descent;

72.play: And meaner than myself have had like fortune.

73.play: But as this title honours me and mine,

74.play: So your dislike, to whom I would be pleasing,

75.play: Doth cloud my joys with danger and with sorrow.

76.play: KING EDWARD IV: My love, forbear to fawn upon their frowns:

77.play: What danger or what sorrow can befall thee,

78.play: So long as Edward is thy constant friend,

79.play: And their true sovereign, whom they must obey?

80.play: Nay, whom they shall obey, and love thee too,

81.play: Unless they seek for hatred at my hands;

82.play: Which if they do, yet will I keep thee safe,

83.play: And they shall feel the vengeance of my wrath.

84.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside]  I hear, yet say not much, but think the more.

85.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now, messenger, what letters or what news

87.play: Go to, we pardon thee: therefore, in brief,

88.play: Tell me their words as near as thou canst guess them.

89.play: What answer makes King Lewis unto our letters?

90.play: Is Lewis so brave? belike he thinks me Henry.

91.play: But what said Lady Bona to my marriage?

92.play: I blame not her, she could say little less;

93.play: She had the wrong. But what said Henry's queen?

94.play: For I have heard that she was there in place.

95.play: Belike she minds to play the Amazon.

96.play: But what said Warwick to these injuries?

97.play: Ha! durst the traitor breathe out so proud words?

98.play: Well I will arm me, being thus forewarn'd:

99.play: They shall have wars and pay for their presumption.

100.play: But say, is Warwick friends with Margaret?

101.play: CLARENCE: Belike the elder; Clarence will have the younger.

102.play: Now, brother king, farewell, and sit you fast,

103.play: For I will hence to Warwick's other daughter;

104.play: That, though I want a kingdom, yet in marriage

105.play: I may not prove inferior to yourself.

106.play: You that love me and Warwick, follow me.

108.play: My thoughts aim at a further matter; I

109.play: Stay not for the love of Edward, but the crown.

110.play: KING EDWARD IV: Clarence and Somerset both gone to Warwick!

111.play: Yet am I arm'd against the worst can happen;

112.play: And haste is needful in this desperate case.

113.play: Pembroke and Stafford, you in our behalf

114.play: Go levy men, and make prepare for war;

115.play: They are already, or quickly will be landed:

116.play: Myself in person will straight follow you.

117.play: But, ere I go, Hastings and Montague,

118.play: Resolve my doubt. You twain, of all the rest,

119.play: Are near to Warwick by blood and by alliance:

120.play: Tell me if you love Warwick more than me?

121.play: If it be so, then both depart to him;

122.play: I rather wish you foes than hollow friends:

123.play: But if you mind to hold your true obedience,

124.play: Give me assurance with some friendly vow,

125.play: That I may never have you in suspect.

126.play: MONTAGUE: So God help Montague as he proves true!

127.play: HASTINGS: And Hastings as he favours Edward's cause!

128.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now, brother Richard, will you stand by us?

129.play: GLOUCESTER: Ay, in despite of all that shall withstand you.

130.play: KING EDWARD IV: Why, so! then am I sure of victory.

131.play: Now therefore let us hence; and lose no hour,

132.play: Till we meet Warwick with his foreign power.

1.play: Enter WARWICK and OXFORD, with French soldiers

2.play: WARWICK: Trust me, my lord, all hitherto goes well;

3.play: The common people by numbers swarm to us.

4.play: But see where Somerset and Clarence come!

5.play: Speak suddenly, my lords, are we all friends?

6.play: CLARENCE: Fear not that, my lord.

7.play: WARWICK: Then, gentle Clarence, welcome unto Warwick;

8.play: And welcome, Somerset: I hold it cowardice

9.play: To rest mistrustful where a noble heart

10.play: Hath pawn'd an open hand in sign of love;

11.play: Else might I think that Clarence, Edward's brother,

12.play: Were but a feigned friend to our proceedings:

13.play: But welcome, sweet Clarence; my daughter shall be thine.

14.play: And now what rests but, in night's coverture,

15.play: Thy brother being carelessly encamp'd,

16.play: His soldiers lurking in the towns about,

17.play: And but attended by a simple guard,

18.play: We may surprise and take him at our pleasure?

19.play: Our scouts have found the adventure very easy:

20.play: That as Ulysses and stout Diomede

21.play: With sleight and manhood stole to Rhesus' tents,

22.play: And brought from thence the Thracian fatal steeds,

23.play: So we, well cover'd with the night's black mantle,

24.play: At unawares may beat down Edward's guard

25.play: And seize himself; I say not, slaughter him,

26.play: For I intend but only to surprise him.

27.play: You that will follow me to this attempt,

28.play: Applaud the name of Henry with your leader.

29.play: Why, then, let's on our way in silent sort:

30.play: For Warwick and his friends, God and Saint George!

1.play: Enter three Watchmen, to guard KING EDWARD IV's tent

2.play: Enter WARWICK, CLARENCE, OXFORD, SOMERSET, and French soldiers, silent all

3.play: WARWICK: This is his tent; and see where stand his guard.

4.play: Courage, my masters! honour now or never!

5.play: But follow me, and Edward shall be ours.

6.play: SOMERSET: What are they that fly there?

7.play: WARWICK: Richard and Hastings: let them go; here is The duke.

8.play: KING EDWARD IV: The duke! Why, Warwick, when we parted,

9.play: Thou call'dst me king.

10.play: WARWICK: Ay, but the case is alter'd:

11.play: When you disgraced me in my embassade,

12.play: Then I degraded you from being king,

13.play: And come now to create you Duke of York.

14.play: Alas! how should you govern any kingdom,

15.play: That know not how to use ambassadors,

16.play: Nor how to be contented with one wife,

17.play: Nor how to use your brothers brotherly,

18.play: Nor how to study for the people's welfare,

19.play: Nor how to shroud yourself from enemies?

20.play: KING EDWARD IV: Yea, brother of Clarence, are thou here too?

21.play: Nay, then I see that Edward needs must down.

22.play: Yet, Warwick, in despite of all mischance,

23.play: Of thee thyself and all thy complices,

24.play: Edward will always bear himself as king:

25.play: Though fortune's malice overthrow my state,

26.play: My mind exceeds the compass of her wheel.

27.play: WARWICK: Then, for his mind, be Edward England's king:

28.play: But Henry now shall wear the English crown,

29.play: And be true king indeed, thou but the shadow.

30.play: My Lord of Somerset, at my request,

31.play: See that forthwith Duke Edward be convey'd

32.play: Unto my brother, Archbishop of York.

33.play: When I have fought with Pembroke and his fellows,

34.play: I'll follow you, and tell what answer

35.play: Lewis and the Lady Bona send to him.

36.play: Now, for a while farewell, good Duke of York.

37.play: KING EDWARD IV: What fates impose, that men must needs abide;

38.play: It boots not to resist both wind and tide.

39.play: OXFORD: What now remains, my lords, for us to do

40.play: But march to London with our soldiers?

41.play: WARWICK: Ay, that's the first thing that we have to do;

42.play: To free King Henry from imprisonment

43.play: And see him seated in the regal throne.

1.play: Enter QUEEN ELIZABETH and RIVERS

2.play: RIVERS: Madam, what makes you in this sudden change?

3.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Why brother Rivers, are you yet to learn

4.play: What late misfortune is befall'n King Edward?

5.play: RIVERS: What! loss of some pitch'd battle against Warwick?

6.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: No, but the loss of his own royal person.

7.play: RIVERS: Then is my sovereign slain?

8.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Ay, almost slain, for he is taken prisoner,

9.play: Either betray'd by falsehood of his guard

10.play: Or by his foe surprised at unawares:

11.play: And, as I further have to understand,

12.play: Is new committed to the Bishop of York,

13.play: Fell Warwick's brother and by that our foe.

14.play: RIVERS: These news I must confess are full of grief;

15.play: Yet, gracious madam, bear it as you may:

16.play: Warwick may lose, that now hath won the day.

17.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Till then fair hope must hinder life's decay.

18.play: And I the rather wean me from despair

19.play: For love of Edward's offspring in my womb:

20.play: This is it that makes me bridle passion

21.play: And bear with mildness my misfortune's cross;

22.play: Ay, ay, for this I draw in many a tear

23.play: And stop the rising of blood-sucking sighs,

24.play: Lest with my sighs or tears I blast or drown

25.play: King Edward's fruit, true heir to the English crown.

26.play: RIVERS: But, madam, where is Warwick then become?

27.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: I am inform'd that he comes towards London,

28.play: To set the crown once more on Henry's head:

29.play: Guess thou the rest; King Edward's friends must down,

30.play: But, to prevent the tyrant's violence,--

31.play: For trust not him that hath once broken faith,--

32.play: I'll hence forthwith unto the sanctuary,

33.play: To save at least the heir of Edward's right:

34.play: There shall I rest secure from force and fraud.

35.play: Come, therefore, let us fly while we may fly:

36.play: If Warwick take us we are sure to die.

1.play: Enter GLOUCESTER, HASTINGS, and STANLEY

2.play: GLOUCESTER: Now, my Lord Hastings and Sir William Stanley,

3.play: Leave off to wonder why I drew you hither,

4.play: Into this chiefest thicket of the park.

5.play: Thus stands the case: you know our king, my brother,

6.play: Is prisoner to the bishop here, at whose hands

7.play: He hath good usage and great liberty,

8.play: And, often but attended with weak guard,

9.play: Comes hunting this way to disport himself.

10.play: I have advertised him by secret means

11.play: That if about this hour he make his way

12.play: Under the colour of his usual game,

13.play: He shall here find his friends with horse and men

14.play: To set him free from his captivity.

15.play: KING EDWARD IV: Nay, this way, man: see where the huntsmen stand.

16.play: Now, brother of Gloucester, Lord Hastings, and the rest,

17.play: Stand you thus close, to steal the bishop's deer?

18.play: GLOUCESTER: Brother, the time and case requireth haste:

19.play: Your horse stands ready at the park-corner.

20.play: KING EDWARD IV: But whither shall we then?

21.play: HASTINGS: To Lynn, my lord,

22.play: And ship from thence to Flanders.

23.play: GLOUCESTER: Well guess'd, believe me; for that was my meaning.

24.play: KING EDWARD IV: Stanley, I will requite thy forwardness.

25.play: GLOUCESTER: But wherefore stay we? 'tis no time to talk.

26.play: KING EDWARD IV: Huntsman, what say'st thou? wilt thou go along?

27.play: GLOUCESTER: Come then, away; let's ha' no more ado.

28.play: KING EDWARD IV: Bishop, farewell: shield thee from Warwick's frown;

29.play: And pray that I may repossess the crown.

1.play: Flourish. Enter KING HENRY VI, CLARENCE, WARWICK,  SOMERSET, HENRY OF RICHMOND, OXFORD, MONTAGUE, and Lieutenant of the Tower

2.play: KING HENRY VI: Master lieutenant, now that God and friends

3.play: Have shaken Edward from the regal seat,

4.play: And turn'd my captive state to liberty,

5.play: My fear to hope, my sorrows unto joys,

6.play: At our enlargement what are thy due fees?

7.play: For what, lieutenant? for well using me?

8.play: Nay, be thou sure I'll well requite thy kindness,

9.play: For that it made my imprisonment a pleasure;

10.play: Ay, such a pleasure as incaged birds

11.play: Conceive when after many moody thoughts

12.play: At last by notes of household harmony

13.play: They quite forget their loss of liberty.

14.play: But, Warwick, after God, thou set'st me free,

15.play: And chiefly therefore I thank God and thee;

16.play: He was the author, thou the instrument.

17.play: Therefore, that I may conquer fortune's spite

18.play: By living low, where fortune cannot hurt me,

19.play: And that the people of this blessed land

20.play: May not be punish'd with my thwarting stars,

21.play: Warwick, although my head still wear the crown,

22.play: I here resign my government to thee,

23.play: For thou art fortunate in all thy deeds.

24.play: WARWICK: Your grace hath still been famed for virtuous;

25.play: And now may seem as wise as virtuous,

26.play: By spying and avoiding fortune's malice,

27.play: For few men rightly temper with the stars:

28.play: Yet in this one thing let me blame your grace,

29.play: For choosing me when Clarence is in place.

30.play: CLARENCE: No, Warwick, thou art worthy of the sway,

31.play: To whom the heavens in thy nativity

32.play: Adjudged an olive branch and laurel crown,

33.play: As likely to be blest in peace and war;

34.play: And therefore I yield thee my free consent.

35.play: WARWICK: And I choose Clarence only for protector.

36.play: KING HENRY VI: Warwick and Clarence give me both your hands:

37.play: Now join your hands, and with your hands your hearts,

38.play: That no dissension hinder government:

39.play: I make you both protectors of this land,

40.play: While I myself will lead a private life

41.play: And in devotion spend my latter days,

42.play: To sin's rebuke and my Creator's praise.

43.play: WARWICK: What answers Clarence to his sovereign's will?

44.play: CLARENCE: That he consents, if Warwick yield consent;

45.play: For on thy fortune I repose myself.

46.play: WARWICK: Why, then, though loath, yet must I be content:

47.play: We'll yoke together, like a double shadow

48.play: To Henry's body, and supply his place;

49.play: I mean, in bearing weight of government,

50.play: While he enjoys the honour and his ease.

51.play: And, Clarence, now then it is more than needful

52.play: Forthwith that Edward be pronounced a traitor,

53.play: And all his lands and goods be confiscate.

54.play: CLARENCE: What else? and that succession be determined.

55.play: WARWICK: Ay, therein Clarence shall not want his part.

56.play: KING HENRY VI: But, with the first of all your chief affairs,

57.play: Let me entreat, for I command no more,

58.play: That Margaret your queen and my son Edward

59.play: Be sent for, to return from France with speed;

60.play: For, till I see them here, by doubtful fear

61.play: My joy of liberty is half eclipsed.

62.play: CLARENCE: It shall be done, my sovereign, with all speed.

63.play: KING HENRY VI: My Lord of Somerset, what youth is that,

64.play: Of whom you seem to have so tender care?

65.play: SOMERSET: My liege, it is young Henry, earl of Richmond.

66.play: KING HENRY VI: Come hither, England's hope.

68.play: Suggest but truth to my divining thoughts,

69.play: This pretty lad will prove our country's bliss.

70.play: His looks are full of peaceful majesty,

71.play: His head by nature framed to wear a crown,

72.play: His hand to wield a sceptre, and himself

73.play: Likely in time to bless a regal throne.

74.play: Make much of him, my lords, for this is he

75.play: Must help you more than you are hurt by me.

77.play: Unsavoury news! but how made he escape?

78.play: My brother was too careless of his charge.

79.play: But let us hence, my sovereign, to provide

80.play: A salve for any sore that may betide.

81.play: SOMERSET: My lord, I like not of this flight of Edward's;

82.play: For doubtless Burgundy will yield him help,

83.play: And we shall have more wars before 't be long.

85.play: Did glad my heart with hope of this young Richmond,

86.play: So doth my heart misgive me, in these conflicts

87.play: What may befall him, to his harm and ours:

88.play: Therefore, Lord Oxford, to prevent the worst,

89.play: Forthwith we'll send him hence to Brittany,

90.play: Till storms be past of civil enmity.

91.play: OXFORD: Ay, for if Edward repossess the crown,

92.play: 'Tis like that Richmond with the rest shall down.

93.play: SOMERSET: It shall be so; he shall to Brittany.

94.play: Come, therefore, let's about it speedily.

1.play: Flourish. Enter KING EDWARD IV, GLOUCESTER, HASTINGS, and Soldiers

2.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now, brother Richard, Lord Hastings, and the rest,

3.play: Yet thus far fortune maketh us amends,

4.play: And says that once more I shall interchange

5.play: My waned state for Henry's regal crown.

6.play: Well have we pass'd and now repass'd the seas

7.play: And brought desired help from Burgundy:

8.play: What then remains, we being thus arrived

9.play: From Ravenspurgh haven before the gates of York,

10.play: But that we enter, as into our dukedom?

11.play: GLOUCESTER: The gates made fast! Brother, I like not this;

12.play: For many men that stumble at the threshold

13.play: Are well foretold that danger lurks within.

14.play: KING EDWARD IV: Tush, man, abodements must not now affright us:

15.play: By fair or foul means we must enter in,

16.play: For hither will our friends repair to us.

17.play: HASTINGS: My liege, I'll knock once more to summon them.

18.play: KING EDWARD IV: But, master mayor, if Henry be your king,

19.play: Yet Edward at the least is Duke of York.

20.play: Why, and I challenge nothing but my dukedom,

21.play: As being well content with that alone.

22.play: GLOUCESTER: [Aside]  But when the fox hath once got in his nose,

23.play: He'll soon find means to make the body follow.

24.play: HASTINGS: Why, master mayor, why stand you in a doubt?

25.play: Open the gates; we are King Henry's friends.

26.play: GLOUCESTER: A wise stout captain, and soon persuaded!

27.play: HASTINGS: The good old man would fain that all were well,

28.play: So 'twere not 'long of him; but being enter'd,

29.play: I doubt not, I, but we shall soon persuade

30.play: Both him and all his brothers unto reason.

31.play: KING EDWARD IV: So, master mayor: these gates must not be shut

32.play: But in the night or in the time of war.

33.play: What! fear not, man, but yield me up the keys;

34.play: For Edward will defend the town and thee,

35.play: And all those friends that deign to follow me.

36.play: GLOUCESTER: Brother, this is Sir John Montgomery,

37.play: Our trusty friend, unless I be deceived.

38.play: KING EDWARD IV: Welcome, Sir John! But why come you in arms?

39.play: MONTAGUE: To help King Edward in his time of storm,

40.play: As every loyal subject ought to do.

41.play: KING EDWARD IV: Thanks, good Montgomery; but we now forget

42.play: Our title to the crown and only claim

43.play: Our dukedom till God please to send the rest.

44.play: MONTAGUE: Then fare you well, for I will hence again:

45.play: I came to serve a king and not a duke.

46.play: Drummer, strike up, and let us march away.

47.play: KING EDWARD IV: Nay, stay, Sir John, awhi le, and we'll debate

48.play: By what safe means the crown may be recover'd.

49.play: MONTAGUE: What talk you of debating? in few words,

50.play: If you'll not here proclaim yourself our king,

51.play: I'll leave you to your fortune and be gone

52.play: To keep them back that come to succor you:

53.play: Why shall we fight, if you pretend no title?

54.play: GLOUCESTER: Why, brother, wherefore stand you on nice points?

55.play: KING EDWARD IV: When we grow stronger, then we'll make our claim:

56.play: Till then, 'tis wisdom to conceal our meaning.

57.play: HASTINGS: Away with scrupulous wit! now arms must rule.

58.play: GLOUCESTER: And fearless minds climb soonest unto crowns.

59.play: Brother, we will proclaim you out of hand:

60.play: The bruit thereof will bring you many friends.

61.play: KING EDWARD IV: Then be it as you will; for 'tis my right,

62.play: And Henry but usurps the diadem.

63.play: MONTAGUE: Ay, now my sovereign speaketh like himself;

64.play: And now will I be Edward's champion.

65.play: HASTINGS: Sound trumpet; Edward shall be here proclaim'd:

67.play: MONTAGUE: And whosoe'er gainsays King Edward's right,

68.play: By this I challenge him to single fight.

69.play: KING EDWARD IV: Thanks, brave Montgomery; and thanks unto you all:

70.play: If fortune serve me, I'll requite this kindness.

71.play: Now, for this night, let's harbour here in York;

72.play: And when the morning sun shall raise his car

73.play: Above the border of this horizon,

74.play: We'll forward towards Warwick and his mates;

75.play: For well I wot that Henry is no soldier.

76.play: Ah, froward Clarence! how evil it beseems thee

77.play: To flatter Henry and forsake thy brother!

78.play: Yet, as we may, we'll meet both thee and Warwick.

79.play: Come on, brave soldiers: doubt not of the day,

80.play: And, that once gotten, doubt not of large pay.

1.play: Flourish. Enter KING HENRY VI, WARWICK, MONTAGUE, CLARENCE, EXETER, and OXFORD

2.play: WARWICK: What counsel, lords? Edward from Belgia,

3.play: With hasty Germans and blunt Hollanders,

4.play: Hath pass'd in safety through the narrow seas,

5.play: And with his troops doth march amain to London;

6.play: And many giddy people flock to him.

7.play: KING HENRY VI: Let's levy men, and beat him back again.

8.play: CLARENCE: A little fire is quickly trodden out;

9.play: Which, being suffer'd, rivers cannot quench.

10.play: WARWICK: In Warwickshire I have true-hearted friends,

11.play: Not mutinous in peace, yet bold in war;

12.play: Those will I muster up: and thou, son Clarence,

13.play: Shalt stir up in Suffolk, Norfolk, and in Kent,

14.play: The knights and gentlemen to come with thee:

15.play: Thou, brother Montague, in Buckingham,

16.play: Northampton and in Leicestershire, shalt find

17.play: Men well inclined to hear what thou command'st:

18.play: And thou, brave Oxford, wondrous well beloved,

19.play: In Oxfordshire shalt muster up thy friends.

20.play: My sovereign, with the loving citizens,

21.play: Like to his island girt in with the ocean,

22.play: Or modest Dian circled with her nymphs,

23.play: Shall rest in London till we come to him.

24.play: Fair lords, take leave and stand not to reply.

25.play: Farewell, my sovereign.

26.play: KING HENRY VI: Farewell, my Hector, and my Troy's true hope.

27.play: CLARENCE: In sign of truth, I kiss your highness' hand.

28.play: KING HENRY VI: Well-minded Clarence, be thou fortunate!

29.play: MONTAGUE: Comfort, my lord; and so I take my leave.

30.play: OXFORD: And thus I seal my truth, and bid adieu.

31.play: KING HENRY VI: Sweet Oxford, and my loving Montague,

32.play: And all at once, once more a happy farewell.

33.play: WARWICK: Farewell, sweet lords: let's meet at Coventry.

34.play: KING HENRY VI: Here at the palace I will rest awhile.

35.play: Cousin of Exeter, what thinks your lordship?

36.play: Methinks the power that Edward hath in field

37.play: Should not be able to encounter mine.

38.play: EXETER: The doubt is that he will seduce the rest.

39.play: KING HENRY VI: That's not my fear; my meed hath got me fame:

40.play: I have not stopp'd mine ears to their demands,

41.play: Nor posted off their suits with slow delays;

42.play: My pity hath been balm to heal their wounds,

43.play: My mildness hath allay'd their swelling griefs,

44.play: My mercy dried their water-flowing tears;

45.play: I have not been desirous of their wealth,

46.play: Nor much oppress'd them with great subsidies.

47.play: Nor forward of revenge, though they much err'd:

48.play: Then why should they love Edward more than me?

49.play: No, Exeter, these graces challenge grace:

50.play: And when the lion fawns upon the lamb,

51.play: The lamb will never cease to follow him.

52.play: EXETER: Hark, hark, my lord! what shouts are these?

53.play: KING EDWARD IV: Seize on the shame-faced Henry, bear him hence;

54.play: And once again proclaim us King of England.

55.play: You are the fount that makes small brooks to flow:

56.play: Now stops thy spring; my sea sha$l suck them dry,

57.play: And swell so much the higher by their ebb.

58.play: Hence with him to the Tower; let him not speak.

59.play: And, lords, towards Coventry bend we our course

61.play: The sun shines hot; and, if we use delay,

62.play: Cold biting winter mars our hoped-for hay.

63.play: GLOUCESTER: Away betimes, before his forces join,

64.play: And take the great-grown traitor unawares:

65.play: Brave warriors, march amain towards Coventry.

1.play: Enter WARWICK, the Mayor of Coventry, two Messengers, and others upon the walls

2.play: WARWICK: Where is the post that came from valiant Oxford?

3.play: How far hence is thy lord, mine honest fellow?

4.play: First Messenger: By this at Dunsmore, marching hitherward.

5.play: WARWICK: How far off is our brother Montague?

6.play: Where is the post that came from Montague?

7.play: Second Messenger: By this at Daintry, with a puissant troop.

8.play: WARWICK: Say, Somerville, what says my loving son?

9.play: And, by thy guess, how nigh is Clarence now?

10.play: SOMERSET: At Southam I did leave him with his forces,

11.play: And do expect him here some two hours hence .

12.play: WARWICK: Then Clarence is at hand, I hear his drum.

13.play: SOMERSET: It is not his, my lord; here Southam lies:

14.play: The drum your honour hears marcheth from Warwick.

15.play: WARWICK: Who should that be? belike, unlook'd-for friends.

16.play: SOMERSET: They are at hand, and you shall quickly know.

17.play: KING EDWARD IV: Go, trumpet, to the walls, and sound a parle .

18.play: GLOUCESTER: See how the surly Warwick mans the wall!

19.play: WARWICK: O unbid spite! is sportful Edward come?

20.play: Where slept our scouts, or how are they seduced,

21.play: That we could hear no news of his repair ?

22.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now, Warwick, wilt thou ope the city gates,

23.play: Speak gentle words and humbly bend thy knee,

24.play: Call Edward king and at his hands beg mercy?

25.play: And he shall pardon thee these outrages.

26.play: WARWICK: Nay, rather, wilt thou draw thy forces hence ,

27.play: Confess who set thee up and pluck'd thee own,

28.play: Call Warwick patron and be penitent?

29.play: And thou shalt still remain the Duke of York.

30.play: GLOUCESTER: I thought , at least, he would have said the king;

31.play: Or did he make the jest against his will?

32.play: WARWICK: Is not a dukedom, sir, a goodly gift?

33.play: GLOUCESTER: Ay, by my faith, for a poor earl to give:

34.play: I'll do thee service for so good a gift.

35.play: WARWICK: 'Twas I that gave the kingdom to thy brother.

36.play: KING EDWARD IV: Why then 'tis mine, if but by Warwick's gift.

37.play: WARWICK: Thou art no Atlas for so great a weight:

38.play: And weakling, Warwick takes his gift again;

39.play: And Henry is my king, Warwick his subject.

40.play: KING EDWARD IV: But Warwick's king is Edward's prisoner:

41.play: And, gallant Warwick, do but answer this:

42.play: What is the body when the head is off?

43.play: GLOUCESTER: Alas, that Warwick had no more forecast,

44.play: But, whiles he thought to steal the single ten,

45.play: The king was slily finger'd from the deck !

46.play: You left poor Henry at the Bishop's palace,

47.play: And, ten to one, you'll meet him in the Tower.

48.play: EDWARD: 'Tis even so; yet you are Warwick still.

49.play: GLOUCESTER: Come, Warwick, take the time; kneel down, kneel down:

50.play: Nay, when? strike now, or else the iron cools.

51.play: WARWICK: I had rather chop this hand off at a blow ,

52.play: And with the other fling it at thy face,

53.play: Than bear so low a sail, to strike to thee.

54.play: KING EDWARD IV: Sail how thou canst, have wind and tide thy friend ,

55.play: This hand, fast wound about thy coal-black hair

56.play: Shall, whiles thy head is warm and new cut off,

57.play: Write in the dust this sentence with thy blood,

58.play: 'Wind-changing Warwick now can change no more.'

59.play: WARWICK: O cheerful colours! see where Oxford comes!

61.play: GLOUCESTER: The gates are open , let us enter too.

62.play: KING EDWARD IV: So other foes may set upon our backs.

63.play: Stand we in good array; for they no doubt

64.play: Will issue out again and bid us battle :

65.play: If not, the city being but of small defence ,

66.play: We'll quickly rouse the traitors in the same.

67.play: WARWICK: O, welcome, Oxford! for we want thy help.

69.play: GLOUCESTER: Thou and thy brother both shall buy this treason

70.play: Even with the dearest blood your bodies bear.

71.play: KING EDWARD IV: The harder match 'd, the greater victory:

72.play: My mind presageth happy gain and conquest.

74.play: GLOUCESTER: Two of thy name, both Dukes of Somerset,

75.play: Have sold their lives unto the house of York;

76.play: And thou shalt be the third if this sword hold.

77.play: WARWICK: And lo, where George of Clarence sweeps along,

78.play: Of force enough to bid his brother battle ;

79.play: With whom an upright zeal to right prevails

80.play: More than the nature of a brother's love!

81.play: Come, Clarence, come; thou wilt, if Warwick call.

82.play: CLARENCE: Father of Warwick, know you what this means?

83.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now welcome more, and ten times more beloved,

84.play: Than if thou never hadst deserved our hate.

85.play: GLOUCESTER: Welcome, good Clarence; this is brotherlike.

86.play: WARWICK: O passing traitor, perjured and unjust !

87.play: KING EDWARD IV: What, Warwick, wilt thou leave the town and fight?

88.play: Or shall we beat the stones about thine ears?

89.play: WARWICK: Alas, I am not coop'd here for defence !

90.play: I will away towards Barnet presently,

91.play: And bid thee battle , Edward, if thou darest.

92.play: KING EDWARD IV: Yes, Warwick, Edward dares, and leads the way.

93.play: Lords, to the field; Saint George and victory!

1.play: Alarum and excursions. Enter KING EDWARD IV, bringing forth WARWICK wounded

2.play: KING EDWARD IV: So, lie thou there: die thou, and die our fear;

3.play: For Warwick was a bug that fear'd us all.

4.play: Now, Montague, sit fast ; I seek for thee,

5.play: That Warwick's bones may keep thine company.

6.play: WARWICK: Ah, who is nigh? come to me, friend or foe,

7.play: And tell me who is victor, York or Warwick?

8.play: Why ask I that? my mangled body shows,

9.play: My blood, my want of strength, my sick heart shows.

10.play: That I must yield my body to the earth

11.play: And, by my fall, the conquest to my foe.

12.play: Thus yields the cedar to the axe's edge,

13.play: Whose arms gave shelter to the princely eagle,

14.play: Under whose shade the ramping lion slept,

15.play: Whose top-branch overpeer'd Jove's spreading tree

16.play: And kept low shrubs from winter's powerful wind.

17.play: These eyes, that now are dimm'd with death's black veil,

18.play: Have been as piercing as the mid-day sun,

19.play: To search the secret treasons of the world:

20.play: The wrinkles in my brows, now filled with blood,

21.play: Were liken'd oft to kingly sepulchres;

22.play: For who lived king, but I could dig his grave ?

23.play: And who durst mine when Warwick bent his brow?

24.play: Lo, now my glory smear'd in dust and blood!

25.play: My parks, my walks, my manors that I had.

26.play: Even now forsake me, and of all my lands

27.play: Is nothing left me but my body's length.

28.play: Why, what is pomp, rule, reign, but earth and dust?

29.play: And, live we how we can, yet die we must.

30.play: SOMERSET: Ah, Warwick, Warwick! wert thou as we are.

31.play: We might recover all our loss again;

32.play: The queen from France hath brought a puissant power :

33.play: Even now we heard the news: ah, could'st thou fly!

34.play: WARWICK: Why, then I would not fly. Ah, Montague,

35.play: If thou be there, sweet brother, take my hand.

36.play: And with thy lips keep in my soul awhile!

37.play: Thou lovest me not; for, brother, if thou didst,

38.play: Thy tears would wash this cold congealed blood

39.play: That glues my lips and will not let me speak.

40.play: Come quickly, Montague, or I am dead.

41.play: SOMERSET: Ah, Warwick! Montague hath breathed his last;

42.play: And to the latest gasp cried out for Warwick,

43.play: And said 'Commend me to my valiant brother.'

44.play: And more he would have said, and more he spoke,

45.play: Which sounded like a clamour in a vault,

46.play: That mought not be distinguished; but at last

47.play: I well might hear, delivered with a groan,

48.play: 'O, farewell, Warwick!'

49.play: WARWICK: Sweet rest his soul! Fly, lords, and save yourselves;

50.play: For Warwick bids you all farewell to meet in heaven.

51.play: OXFORD: Away, away, to meet the queen's great power !

1.play: Flourish. Enter KING EDWARD IV in triumph; with GLOUCESTER, CLARENCE, and the rest

2.play: KING EDWARD IV: Thus far our fortune keeps an upward course,

3.play: And we are graced with wreaths of victory.

4.play: But, in the midst of this bright-shining day,

5.play: I spy a black, suspicious, threatening cloud,

6.play: That will encounter with our glorious sun,

7.play: Ere he attain his easeful western bed:

8.play: I mean , my lords, those powers that the queen

9.play: Hath raised in Gallia have arrived our coast

10.play: And, as we hear, march on to fight with us.

11.play: CLARENCE: A little gale will soon disperse that cloud

12.play: And blow it to the source from whence it came:

13.play: The very beams will dry those vapours up,

14.play: For every cloud engenders not a storm.

15.play: GLOUCESTER: The queen is valued thirty thousand strong,

16.play: And Somerset, with Oxford fled to her:

17.play: If she have time to breathe be well assured

18.play: Her faction will be full as strong as ours.

19.play: KING EDWARD IV: We are advertised by our loving friends

20.play: That they do hold their course toward Tewksbury:

21.play: We, having now the best at Barnet field,

22.play: Will thither straight , for willingness rids way;

23.play: And, as we march, our strength will be augmented

24.play: In every county as we go along.

25.play: Strike up the drum; cry 'Courage!' and away.

1.play: March. Enter QUEEN MARGARET, PRINCE EDWARD, SOMERSET, OXFORD, and soldiers

2.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Great lords, wise men ne'er sit and wail their loss,

3.play: But cheerly seek how to redress their harms.

4.play: What though the mast be now blown overboard,

5.play: The cable broke , the holding-anchor lost,

6.play: And half our sailors swallow'd in the flood?

7.play: Yet lives our pilot still. Is't meet that he

8.play: Should leave the helm and like a fearful lad

9.play: With tearful eyes add water to the sea

10.play: And give more strength to that which hath too much,

11.play: Whiles , in his moan, the ship splits on the rock,

12.play: Which industry and courage might have saved?

13.play: Ah, what a shame ! ah, what a fault were this!

14.play: Say Warwick was our anchor ; what of that?

15.play: And Montague our topmost; what of him?

16.play: Our slaughter'd friends the tackles; what of these?

17.play: Why, is not Oxford here another anchor ?

18.play: And Somerset another goodly mast?

19.play: The friends of France our shrouds and tacklings?

20.play: And, though unskilful, why not Ned and I

21.play: For once allow 'd the skilful pilot's charge?

22.play: We will not from the helm to sit and weep,

23.play: But keep our course, though the rough wind say no,

24.play: From shelves and rocks that threaten us with wreck.

25.play: As good to chide the waves as speak them fair.

26.play: And what is Edward but ruthless sea?

27.play: What Clarence but a quicksand of deceit?

28.play: And Richard but a ragged fatal rock?

29.play: All these the enemies to our poor bark.

30.play: Say you can swim; alas, 'tis but a while!

31.play: Tread on the sand; why, there you quickly sink:

32.play: Bestride the rock; the tide will wash you off,

33.play: Or else you famish; that's a threefold death.

34.play: This speak I, lords, to let you understand,

35.play: If case some one of you would fly from us,

36.play: That there's no hoped-for mercy with the brothers

37.play: More than with ruthless waves, with sands and rocks.

38.play: Why, courage then! what cannot be avoided

39.play: 'Twere childish weakness to lament or fear.

40.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Methinks a woman of this valiant spirit

41.play: Should, if a coward heard her speak these words,

42.play: Infuse his breast with magnanimity

43.play: And make him, naked, foil a man at arms.

44.play: I speak not this as doubting any here

45.play: For did I but suspect a fearful man

46.play: He should have leave to go away betimes,

47.play: Lest in our need he might infect another

48.play: And make him of like spirit to himself.

49.play: If any such be here--as God forbid !--

50.play: Let him depart before we need his help.

51.play: OXFORD: Women and children of so high a courage,

52.play: And warriors faint! why, 'twere perpetual shame .

53.play: O brave young prince! thy famous grandfather

54.play: Doth live again in thee: long mayst thou live

55.play: To bear his image and renew his glories!

56.play: SOMERSET: And he that will not fight for such a hope.

57.play: Go home to bed, and like the owl by day,

58.play: If he arise, be mock'd and wonder'd at.

59.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Thanks, gentle Somerset; sweet Oxford, thanks.

60.play: PRINCE EDWARD: And take his thanks that yet hath nothing else.

61.play: Messenger: Prepare you, lords, for Edward is at hand.

62.play: Ready to fight; therefore be resolute.

63.play: OXFORD: I thought no less: it is his policy

64.play: To haste thus fast , to find us unprovided.

65.play: SOMERSET: But he's deceived; we are in readiness.

66.play: QUEEN MARGARET: This cheers my heart, to see your forwardness.

67.play: OXFORD: Here pitch our battle ; hence we will not budge.

68.play: KING EDWARD IV: Brave followers, yonder stands the thorny wood ,

69.play: Which, by the heavens' assistance and your strength,

70.play: Must by the roots be hewn up yet ere night.

71.play: I need not add more fuel to your fire,

72.play: For well I wot ye blaze to burn them out

73.play: Give signal to the fight, and to it, lords!

74.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Lords, knights, and gentlemen, what I should say

75.play: My tears gainsay; for every word I speak,

76.play: Ye see, I drink the water of mine eyes.

77.play: Therefore, no more but this: Henry, your sovereign,

78.play: Is prisoner to the foe; his state usurp'd,

79.play: His realm a slaughter-house, his subjects slain,

80.play: His statutes cancell'd and his treasure spent;

81.play: And yonder is the wolf that makes this spoil.

82.play: You fight in justice: then, in God's name, lords,

83.play: Be valiant and give signal to the fight.

1.play: Flourish. Enter KING EDWARD IV, GLOUCESTER, CLARENCE, and soldiers; with QUEEN MARGARET, OXFORD, and SOMERSET, prisoners

2.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now here a period of tumultuous broils.

3.play: Away with Oxford to Hames Castle straight :

4.play: For Somerset, off with his guilty head.

5.play: Go, bear them hence ; I will not hear them speak.

6.play: OXFORD: For my part, I'll not trouble thee with words.

7.play: SOMERSET: Nor I, but stoop with patience to my fortune.

8.play: QUEEN MARGARET: So part we sadly in this troublous world,

9.play: To meet with joy in sweet Jerusalem.

10.play: KING EDWARD IV: Is proclamation made, that who finds Edward

11.play: Shall have a high reward, and he his life?

12.play: GLOUCESTER: It is: and lo, where youthful Edward comes!

13.play: KING EDWARD IV: Bring forth the gallant, let us hear him speak.

14.play: What! can so young a thorn begin to prick ?

15.play: Edward, what satisfaction canst thou make

16.play: For bearing arms, for stirring up my subjects,

17.play: And all the trouble thou hast turn 'd me to?

18.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Speak like a subject, proud ambitious York!

19.play: Suppose that I am now my father's mouth;

20.play: Resign thy chair, and where I stand kneel thou,

21.play: Whilst I propose the selfsame words to thee,

22.play: Which traitor, thou wouldst have me answer to.

23.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Ah, that thy father had been so resolved!

24.play: GLOUCESTER: That you might still have worn the petticoat,

25.play: And ne'er have stol'n the breech from Lancaster.

26.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Let AEsop fable in a winter's night;

27.play: His currish riddles sort not with this place.

28.play: GLOUCESTER: By heaven, brat, I'll plague ye for that word.

29.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Ay, thou wast born to be a plague to men.

30.play: GLOUCESTER: For God's sake, take away this captive scold.

31.play: PRINCE EDWARD: Nay, take away this scolding crookback rather.

32.play: KING EDWARD IV: Peace, wilful boy, or I will charm your tongue.

33.play: CLARENCE: Untutor'd lad, thou art too malapert.

34.play: PRINCE EDWARD: I know my duty; you are all undutiful:

35.play: Lascivious Edward, and thou perjured George,

36.play: And thou mis-shapen Dick, I tell ye all

37.play: I am your better, traitors as ye are:

38.play: And thou usurp'st my father's right and mine.

39.play: KING EDWARD IV: Take that, thou likeness of this railer here.

40.play: GLOUCESTER: Sprawl'st thou? take that, to end thy agony.

41.play: CLARENCE: And there's for twitting me with perjury.

42.play: QUEEN MARGARET: O, kill me too!

43.play: GLOUCESTER: Marry, and shall.

44.play: KING EDWARD IV: Hold, Richard, hold; for we have done too much.

45.play: GLOUCESTER: Why should she live, to fill the world with words?

46.play: KING EDWARD IV: What, doth she swoon? use means for her recovery.

47.play: GLOUCESTER: Clarence, excuse me to the king my brother;

48.play: I'll hence to London on a serious matter:

49.play: Ere ye come there, be sure to hear some news.

50.play: CLARENCE: What ? what?

51.play: GLOUCESTER: The Tower, the Tower.

52.play: QUEEN MARGARET: O Ned, sweet Ned! speak to thy mother, boy!

53.play: Canst thou not speak? O traitors! murderers!

54.play: They that stabb'd Caesar shed no blood at all,

55.play: Did not offend, nor were not worthy blame,

56.play: If this foul deed were by to equal it:

57.play: He was a man; this, in respect , a child:

58.play: And men ne'er spend their fury on a child.

59.play: What's worse than murderer, that I may name it?

60.play: No, no, my heart will burst , and if I speak:

61.play: And I will speak, that so my heart may burst .

63.play: How sweet a plant have you untimely cropp'd!

64.play: You have no children, butchers! if you had,

65.play: The thought of them would have stirr'd up remorse :

66.play: But if you ever chance to have a child,

67.play: Look in his youth to have him so cut off

68.play: As, deathmen, you have rid this sweet young prince!

69.play: KING EDWARD IV: Away with her; go, bear her hence perforce.

70.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Nay, never bear me hence , dispatch me here,

71.play: Here sheathe thy sword, I'll pardon thee my death:

72.play: What, wilt thou not? then, Clarence, do it thou.

73.play: CLARENCE: By heaven, I will not do thee so much ease.

74.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Good Clarence, do; sweet Clarence, do thou do it.

75.play: CLARENCE: Didst thou not hear me swear I would not do it?

76.play: QUEEN MARGARET: Ay, but thou usest to forswear thyself:

78.play: What, wilt thou not? Where is that devil's butcher,

79.play: Hard-favour'd Richard? Richard, where art thou?

80.play: Thou art not here: murder is thy alms-deed;

81.play: Petitioners for blood thou ne'er put 'st back.

82.play: KING EDWARD IV: Away, I say; I charge ye, bear her hence .

83.play: QUEEN MARGARET: So come to you and yours, as to this Prince!

84.play: KING EDWARD IV: Where's Richard gone?

85.play: CLARENCE: To London, all in post; and, as I guess,

86.play: To make a bloody supper in the Tower.

87.play: KING EDWARD IV: He's sudden , if a thing comes in his head.

88.play: Now march we hence : discharge the common sort

89.play: With pay and thanks, and let's away to London

90.play: And see our gentle queen how well she fares:

91.play: By this, I hope, she hath a son for me.

1.play: Enter KING HENRY VI and GLOUCESTER, with the Lieutenant, on the walls

2.play: GLOUCESTER: Good day, my lord. What, at your book so hard?

3.play: KING HENRY VI: Ay, my good lord:--my lord, I should say rather;

4.play: 'Tis sin to flatter; 'good' was little better:

5.play: 'Good Gloucester' and 'good devil' were alike,

6.play: And both preposterous; therefore, not 'good lord.'

7.play: GLOUCESTER: Sirrah, leave us to ourselves: we must confer.

8.play: KING HENRY VI: So flies the reckless shepherd from the wolf;

9.play: So first the harmless sheep doth yield his fleece

10.play: And next his throat unto the butcher's knife.

11.play: What scene of death hath Roscius now to act?

12.play: GLOUCESTER: Suspicion always haunts the guilty mind;

13.play: The thief doth fear each bush an officer.

14.play: KING HENRY VI: The bird that hath been limed in a bush,

15.play: With trembling wings misdoubteth every bush;

16.play: And I, the hapless male to one sweet bird,

17.play: Have now the fatal object in my eye

18.play: Where my poor young was limed, was caught and kill'd.

19.play: GLOUCESTER: Why, what a peevish fool was that of Crete,

20.play: That taught his son the office of a fowl!

21.play: An yet, for all his wings, the fool was drown'd.

22.play: KING HENRY VI: I, Daedalus; my poor boy, Icarus;

23.play: Thy father, Minos, that denied our course;

24.play: The sun that sear 'd the wings of my sweet boy

25.play: Thy brother Edward, and thyself the sea

26.play: Whose envious gulf did swallow up his life.

27.play: Ah, kill me with thy weapon, not with words!

28.play: My breast can better brook thy dagger's point

29.play: Than can my ears that tragic history.

30.play: But wherefore dost thou come? is't for my life?

31.play: GLOUCESTER: Think'st thou I am an executioner?

32.play: KING HENRY VI: A persecutor, I am sure, thou art:

33.play: If murdering innocents be executing,

34.play: Why, then thou art an executioner.

35.play: GLOUCESTER: Thy son I kill'd for his presumption.

36.play: KING HENRY VI: Hadst thou been kill'd when first thou didst presume,

37.play: Thou hadst not lived to kill a son of mine.

38.play: And thus I prophesy, that many a thousand,

39.play: Which now mistrust no parcel of my fear,

40.play: And many an old man's sigh and many a widow 's,

41.play: And many an orphan's water-standing eye--

42.play: Men for their sons, wives for their husbands,

43.play: And orphans for their parents timeless death--

44.play: Shall rue the hour that ever thou wast born.

45.play: The owl shriek'd at thy birth,--an evil sign ;

46.play: The night-crow cried, aboding luckless time;

47.play: Dogs howl'd, and hideous tempest shook down trees;

48.play: The raven rook 'd her on the chimney's top,

49.play: And chattering pies in dismal discords sung.

50.play: Thy mother felt more than a mother's pain,

51.play: And, yet brought forth less than a mother's hope,

52.play: To wit , an indigested and deformed lump,

53.play: Not like the fruit of such a goodly tree.

54.play: Teeth hadst thou in thy head when thou wast born,

55.play: To signify thou camest to bite the world:

56.play: And, if the rest be true which I have heard,

58.play: GLOUCESTER: I'll hear no more: die, prophet in thy speech:

59.play: KING HENRY VI: Ay, and for much more slaughter after this.

60.play: God forgive my sins, and pardon thee!

61.play: GLOUCESTER: What, will the aspiring blood of Lancaster

62.play: Sink in the ground? I thought it would have mounted.

63.play: See how my sword weeps for the poor king's death!

64.play: O, may such purple tears be alway shed

65.play: From those that wish the downfall of our house!

66.play: If any spark of life be yet remaining,

67.play: Down, down to hell; and say I sent thee thither:

1.play: Flourish. Enter KING EDWARD IV, QUEEN ELIZABETH, CLARENCE, GLOUCESTER, HASTINGS, a Nurse with the young Prince, and Attendants

2.play: KING EDWARD IV: Once more we sit in England's royal throne,

3.play: Re-purchased with the blood of enemies.

4.play: What valiant foemen, like to autumn's corn,

5.play: Have we mow 'd down, in tops of all their pride !

6.play: Three Dukes of Somerset, threefold renown'd

7.play: For hardy and undoubted champions;

8.play: Two Cliffords, as the father and the son,

9.play: And two Northumberlands; two braver men

10.play: Ne'er spurr'd their coursers at the trumpet's sound;

11.play: With them, the two brave bears, Warwick and Montague,

12.play: That in their chains fetter'd the kingly lion

13.play: And made the forest tremble when they roar'd.

14.play: Thus have we swept suspicion from our seat

15.play: And made our footstool of security.

16.play: Come hither, Bess, and let me kiss my boy.

17.play: Young Ned, for thee, thine uncles and myself

18.play: Have in our armours watch 'd the winter's night,

19.play: Went all afoot in summer's scalding heat ,

20.play: That thou mightst repossess the crown in peace;

21.play: And of our labours thou shalt reap the gain.

22.play: [Aside]

23.play: Clarence and Gloucester, love my lovely queen;

24.play: And kiss your princely nephew , brothers both.

25.play: CLARENCE: The duty that I owe unto your majesty

26.play: I seal upon the lips of this sweet babe.

27.play: QUEEN ELIZABETH: Thanks, noble Clarence; worthy brother, thanks.

28.play: [Aside]

29.play: KING EDWARD IV: Now am I seated as my soul delights,

30.play: Having my country 's peace and brothers' loves.

31.play: CLARENCE: What will your grace have done with Margaret?

32.play: Reignier, her father, to the king of France

33.play: Hath pawn'd the Sicils and Jerusalem,

34.play: And hither have they sent it for her ransom.

35.play: KING EDWARD IV: Away with her, and waft her hence to France.

36.play: And now what rests but that we spend the time

37.play: With stately triumphs, mirthful comic shows,

38.play: Such as befits the pleasure of the court?

39.play: Sound drums and trumpets! farewell sour annoy!

40.play: For here, I hope, begins our lasting joy.
